actions.thought: With Ace-Three suited on the button in a three-handed game, this is a strong starting hand. I have position and the initiative to take control of the pot. Raising is the standard play to potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. I will raise to 250 to initiate aggression.

J8 offsuit is a weak hand, especially to play out of position from the Small Blind. Facing a button raise, folding is the standard and mathematically correct play to preserve my stack for better spots.

Flashlight raised to 2.5x from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds (requiring 27% equity) to defend my Big Blind, 65 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given it is the first hand of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable situation rather than defending with a marginal hand that will be difficult to realize equity with post-flop.
8s5s is too weak to open from the Button even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve the stack for better spots.

I have King-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind. The action has folded to me, meaning I am heads-up against the Big Blind. KTo is a strong hand in this spot, well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or build it for post-flop play.

Facing a raise from the Small Blind, I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Given the pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), this is a mathematically profitable call. T9o has decent playability and equity against a wide Small Blind opening range, allowing me to see a flop in position relative to the action.

I missed this flop completely with King-Ten offsuit. The board is Ace-high and features a flush draw. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I could represent the Ace with a continuation bet, but Flashlight's range for calling from the Big Blind includes many hands that connected with this board or contain an Ace. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, planning to fold to a significant bet as I have very little equity on this texture.

The flop features an Ace, which heavily connects with the Small Blind's opening range. With Ten-high and no significant draw, my hand has virtually no showdown value and poor equity. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that is likely to contain many Aces, making it a low-EV move. Checking allows me to control the pot size and take a free card, while keeping my range wide and not committing chips to a likely losing hand.

The turn card is the Four of Diamonds, which pairs the board. After checking the flop and seeing Flashlight check back, it's possible neither of us has a strong hand. However, my King-high still has very little equity. I will check again to see if I can reach a cheap showdown or evaluate based on Flashlight's action. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have no improved my hand and have no draw.

The board is now paired with an Ace and a Four. My ten-high hand is extremely weak and lacks significant equity or showdown value. The Small Blind checked twice, which could indicate they are also weak, or potentially trapping. Betting as a pure bluff into a check-check line is high-risk and unlikely to force a fold from an Ace or a pocket pair. Checking back is the most prudent decision to see a river card for free, keeping the pot small and minimizing losses with a marginal hand.

The board has been checked through the flop and turn, suggesting neither of us has a particularly strong hand. My King-high has very little showdown value, but I don't see a compelling reason to bluff here as my line (checking twice after being the pre-flop aggressor) doesn't strongly represent a value hand. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if Flashlight bets, as I cannot beat most of their calling range.

With ten-high, I have effectively zero showdown value. The opponent has checked three streets, which suggests a missed continuation bet or a weak hand that has given up. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to be profitable because it won't force folds from any hand that currently beats me, and the opponent may have an Ace or a small pair. Checking back is the most disciplined move to minimize risk and conclude the hand.

In a three-handed game, K6o is a standard opening hand from the button. It has decent high-card value and allows me to take control of the pot early. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a balanced sizing to apply pressure on the blinds while maintaining a good risk-to-reward ratio for a steal attempt.

Holding Ace-Four offsuit in the Small Blind against a button raise puts me at a significant disadvantage. I am out of position, and the hand is easily dominated by any Ace with a better kicker. Given the early stage of the tournament and the tactical disadvantage of playing this hand out of position, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Defending the Big Blind with a suited gapper like 68s against a Button open is a standard play. We are getting good pot odds to call the extra 150 chips to see a flop, and this hand plays well post-flop with straight and flush possibilities.

The flop comes Ad As Td, which completely misses my 6s 8s and heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. As the out-of-position caller, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. With no pair and no draw, I will be check-folding to any bet.

The flop Ad-As-Td is very static and generally favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. However, my specific hand (K-high) has some showdown value in a three-handed game. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and avoid getting check-raised by a hand that has me beat, such as an Ace or a Ten. This also allows me to see the turn for free and potentially improve or induce a bluff from the opponent later.

The turn puts a second pair on the board (Aces and Tens). My 6s 8s provides virtually no showdown value, as I am essentially playing the board with an 8 kicker. Any opponent with a higher card (9, J, Q, K) or an Ace/Ten has me beat. Although Flash checked the flop, suggesting they might lack an Ace, they still have plenty of hands in their range that beat me. Betting as a bluff could work to fold out some higher cards, but checking and preserving my stack with such a marginal holding is the safer play. I will check and fold to any bet.

The board is double-paired (Ad-As-Td-Ts), meaning any Ace or Ten has a full house. Since I hold a King (Kc), my current best five-card hand is Aces and Tens with a King kicker (A-A-T-T-K). In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand for showdown purposes. If the opponent does not have an Ace or a Ten, I am either winning or chopping (if they also have a King). By checking, I control the pot and get to a cheap showdown, as betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands or fold out better ones.

The river brings a King, making the board Ad As Td Ts Kh. My 6s 8s means my best five-card hand is exactly the board (Two Pair, Aces and Tens, with a King kicker). Since any pocket pair below Tens and any unconnected cards lower than a King will also just play the board, checking allows us to chop the pot against all of Flash's missed high-card or low pocket pair hands. Trying to bluff at this point is risky because Flash's range still includes plenty of Kings, and potentially some slow-played Aces or Tens. I'll check and see if we can get to a cheap showdown to split the pot.

The board is double-paired (Ad-As-Td-Ts) with a King (Kh). My best five-card hand is Aces and Tens with a King kicker (A-A-T-T-K). Since I hold a King (Kc), I am essentially playing the board's best available non-full-house hand. Pro has checked all streets, including this river. If Pro has an Ace or a Ten, they have a full house and I lose. If they do not have an Ace or a Ten, we are likely chopping the pot, as the board provides a very strong hand for everyone. Betting provides no value here because better hands (A or T) will never fold, and there are no worse hands to call me since everyone plays at least the board. Checking back ensures a cheap showdown or a chop.

As the button in this three-handed game, I am first to act pre-flop. Suited connectors like 7d 6d are excellent hands for playing in position. Opening the pot with a raise to 250 chips (2.5x the big blind) is a standard strategy to exert pressure, capture the blinds, or build a pot where I have a positional advantage throughout the hand.

A4 offsuit is too weak to call from the small blind, even against a button open in a 3-handed game. Playing a weak ace out of position post-flop is a losing proposition, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary at this early stage. Folding to preserve the stack is the optimal play.

I am in the Big Blind with 94o. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (150 to call into a pot of 400), 94o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. It does not flop well and has low high-card value. In the early stages of this tournament, it is better to fold such weak hands and wait for better spots rather than defending too wide and playing a difficult pot out of position.
67 offsuit is too weak to play from the button, even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard, most profitable play to conserve the stack for better opportunities.

I have a premium hand (Pocket Tens) in a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folded. In 3-handed play, TT is extremely strong. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot and exert pressure on the Big Blind.

I am defending my Big Blind against an open raise from the Small Blind. While Jack-four suited is a marginal holding, I am receiving favorable pot odds (200 to call for a 400 pot) and I will have the positional advantage of acting last throughout the post-flop betting rounds. Given the deep stacks at this stage of the tournament, calling is the most appropriate play to realize my equity.

The flop of Js Ad Jh is quite coordinated and hits many parts of the Big Blind's defending range, including hands with an Ace or a Jack. My pocket Tens are now a bluff-catcher or a marginal made hand. By checking, I can keep the pot small, avoid bloating it when I might be behind, and see how Flashlight reacts. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me.

With trip Jacks on a board of Js-Ad-Jh, I hold a very strong hand that is currently only losing to pocket Aces. Flash has checked to me, which signals weakness or a trap. I need to start building the pot to extract value from any Ace-high hands or lower pocket pairs that may have continued pre-flop. A bet of 250 into the 600 pot is a balanced approach to gain value while keeping my opponent's range wide.

I have Pocket Tens on a board of Js-Ad-Jh. While the Ace and the paired Jacks are threatening, TT is a strong enough hand to call a single bet on the flop. Flashlight's 250 bet into a 600 pot could represent an Ace or a Jack, but it could also be a positional bet with a wide range of hands including lower pairs or air. Folding now would be overly cautious, as I am still ahead of many hands in the Big Blind's range. Calling allows me to evaluate the turn and keep the pot manageable.

The turn 2c is a total brick and does not change the board texture significantly. My pocket Tens remain a bluff-catcher or a marginal made hand. Since I checked and called on the flop, checking the turn is the best way to control the pot size and gauge the strength of Flashlight's hand. If Flashlight checks back, it's possible I have the best hand against their wider range; if they bet again, I will have to reconsider my position based on the bet sizing.

I hold trip Jacks with a Jack-Four kicker on a board of Js-Ad-Jh-2c. Flash checked the turn, which indicates a likely weak-to-moderate hand, possibly an Ace or a smaller pocket pair. Since I have a very strong holding, I need to continue betting for value to build the pot against potential calling ranges like Ace-x or underpairs. Betting 600 into the 1100 pot provides a good price for my opponent to make a mistake while keeping the pot growing for a potentially larger river bet.

The turn 2c is a brick, but Flashlight's bet of 600 into a 1100 pot is a strong second barrel on an Ace-high, paired board. My pocket Tens are essentially a bluff-catcher here, as I lose to any Ace, any Jack, or any slow-played full house. Because I hold two Tens, I actually block some of the semi-bluffing hands Flashlight could have, such as QT or T9, making a pure bluff less likely. Given the board texture and the bet sizing, it is probable that Flashlight has at least an Ace or better. Continuing would likely lead to an even more difficult decision on the river, so I will fold now to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with T9s, a strong speculative hand that plays very well in position, especially in a 3-handed game. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative, pressure the blinds, and build a pot for a hand that has excellent post-flop potential.

Facing an open raise from the button, A5s is a strong hand that plays well post-flop, making it an excellent candidate for a call from the small blind. I prefer to keep the pot controlled while out of position rather than 3-betting and bloating the pot against an active player. Calling allows me to realize my equity on the flop while maintaining a reasonable stack size.

I am holding 62 offsuit in the big blind, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the button and a call from the small blind, the only correct play is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

The board of 7h-Qc-7d is relatively dry and favors the button's opening range significantly. Holding Ace-high, I have some showdown value against complete air but am effectively bluff-catching. Since I am out of position as the small blind, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and see how my opponent reacts. Betting into the aggressor on this board does not make sense with this weak holding.

As the pre-flop aggressor on the button, I have a significant range advantage on this Queen-high paired board. Flashlight's check from the Small Blind is standard. A continuation bet of about 40% of the pot is effective here to fold out hands that missed the flop (like small pairs or high-card hands without a Queen) and to maintain the initiative. Even if called, I have backdoor diamond equity and overcards.

The opponent has made a small continuation bet of 300 into a 1000 pot. With Ace-high on a 7-Q-7 board, I am effectively bluff-catching. However, the sizing is small enough that I am getting pot odds of roughly 4:1, making a call mathematically sound against a reasonably wide range of hands that are missing this board or overplaying overcards. Folding here would be overly exploitable to standard continuation bets on dry boards.

The opponent, Flash, has bet 300 into a pot that has grown to 1300 on the turn. With Ace-high on a board of 7h-Qc-7d-Jh, my hand is essentially a bluff catcher. However, the sizing of the bet is small, giving me excellent pot odds (around 4:1) to continue. Given the dry texture of the board and my Ace-high which blocks some of the opponent's potential Ax hands, calling is the most mathematically sound decision to realize my equity and see the river, rather than folding to a potentially weak probe bet.

The Jack on the turn provides me with an open-ended straight draw, as either an eight or a King will complete my hand. Given that Flashlight check-called on the paired flop of Seven-Queen-Seven, their range likely consists of a Queen, a Seven, or a pocket pair. Checking back is the most strategic play here; it allows me to realize my equity for free, avoids the risk of a check-raise that would force me off my draw, and keeps the pot manageable while I am still in a drawing position.

With Ace-high on a board of 7h-Qc-7d-Jh-8d, I have absolutely no showdown value against a range that includes any pair, which is highly likely given the pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet from Flash. Flash checked the turn, which could indicate a missed draw or a marginal showdown hand. Attempting a bluff into a range that has already shown strength would be ineffective, and checking gives me the best opportunity to see a showdown if Flash also has a weak hand or missed their overcards.

On the river, the Eight of Diamonds completes my straight (8-9-10-J-Q). Flashlight has checked the river after check-calling the flop and checking the turn, which indicates a range of marginal made hands like a Queen (Qx) or perhaps a pocket pair. Since my straight is well-disguised and highly likely to be the best hand (the only hands beating me are unlikely full houses or a higher straight with King-Ten), I will bet for value. A bet of 850, which is approximately 65% of the pot, is a sizing that can be called by any Queen or potentially a Jack or Eight.

The board of 7h-Qc-7d-Jh-8d is quite wet and interacts well with most opening ranges. My Ace-high (As-5d) has no showdown value, losing to any pair, including the 8s, Js, or Qs that Flash likely has. While Flash checked the turn, the river bet of 850 into a 1200 pot indicates either a thin value bet with a marginal pair or a polar bluff. Given the high probability of the opponent holding at least an 8 or better, and the lack of strength in my own hand, calling is mathematically incorrect and would be a significant leak. I must fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket nines (9d 9s) are a strong premium hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Being on the button, I want to take the initiative and open the betting to build the pot and capitalize on my positional advantage. A standard raise to 250 chips should effectively narrow the field or allow me to play a controlled, in-position hand.
I have Q4 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. This hand is way too weak to call or 3-bet. Folding is the only sensible option to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 3s. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. This is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. Even though I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, Q3o has poor playability and realization of equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
K2o is a very weak hand pre-flop, even on the button in a 3-handed game. It is easily dominated by better kings and has poor playability post-flop. I'll fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I have JJ in the small blind, which is a premium pocket pair, especially in a three-handed game. With the button folding, I am head-to-head with the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure, maintaining consistency with my previous opening sizes in this tournament.

I am holding 8-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even with the position of being in the big blind and getting 2:1 odds to call, there is no strategic value in defending this hand against a raise from the small blind. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Based on previous hands, a raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing for opening the pot. This allows me to take control of the hand early and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position.

89 offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the small blind, especially out of position against an active button raiser. The pot odds do not justify a call with this marginal holding, so folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.
Q2o is a very weak offsuit hand. Even from the Big Blind and facing a button raise, it's far below our defending range. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve our stack.

Eight-five offsuit is a very weak hand with minimal equity and playability. Even on the button, it is not a profitable open. Playing this hand would likely result in losing chips unnecessarily, especially if I face resistance from the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice.

With Flashlight folding on the button, the action is on me in the Small Blind. My hand is 9s 3c, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity. There is no strategic justification to limp or raise with this holding, so folding is the optimal and standard decision.
Holding 2d 8c offsuit on the button is a very weak starting hand. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with this trash hand, so the standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 3d, which is a very weak hand (93o). The Button (Pro) has already folded. While the cost to complete is only 50 chips, I would be playing out of position against Flashlight (Big Blind), who has shown a willingness to defend the blind in previous hands. Raising as a bluff with such a weak hand is also suboptimal given the opponent's tendency to call pre-flop. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 3d 2h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, this hand has extremely poor equity and playability post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the tournament context, there is no reason to attempt a bluff or a steal with such a low-value hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong hand in a heads-up scenario against the Big Blind after the Button folded. Raising is the optimal strategy to exercise aggression, take down the pot pre-flop, or set up a C-bet if the Big Blind calls. 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opponent.

K3o is a weak offsuit king. Even in a blind versus blind situation, facing a 3x raise from the small blind, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify calling. The best and most standard move here is to fold and wait for better spots to commit chips.
KJo is a strong hand in a 3-handed game when on the button. Opening the pot with a raise is standard to seize initiative, apply pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

Facing a button raise from Flashlight with King-Nine suited in the Small Blind. While K9s has some playability and makes good flushes, playing it out of position against a frequent opener is marginal. Calling leaves us vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind, and 3-betting as a bluff has its risks early in the tournament. Folding is the safest and most solid play to preserve our stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 6s. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. While the price to call is relatively small (150 more into a pot of 400), 64 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to preserve my chips for better spots, folding is the most disciplined play here.
I am on the button with 3c 5d, an extremely weak unsuited hand with very poor equity and playability. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although the Button has folded and I only need to contend with the Big Blind, this hand has very little equity and performs poorly post-flop. There is no reason to commit more chips with such a marginal holding.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and want to exert pressure on the blinds. Pro (Big Blind) has been folding frequently to pre-flop raises, and Flashlight (Small Blind) has shown a willingness to call but also to fold post-flop. I will open for my standard 3x raise of 300 to build the pot and potentially take the blinds immediately.

I hold Ace-Nine offsuit in the small blind against a button open. While A9o is a decent hand, playing it out of position can be tricky. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent equity range against the button's opening frequency, keeping the pot manageable. I am committing a total of 300 chips to the pot.

8s 4d offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the button and a call from the small blind, the pot odds do not justify playing this garbage hand out of position. The optimal decision is to fold and preserve the stack.

The board is paired with two Jacks and a four, making it a very dry and disconnected texture. My Ace-high is currently behind most of the Button's range, and since I am out of position, leading into the pre-flop aggressor is not optimal. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and gauge the opponent's strength before deciding whether to continue.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board has come out paired with Jacks (Jh 4h Jd). This board structure generally favors the raiser's range. While I only have King-high (Ks Qh), Flashlight's check provides an opportunity to continuation bet. By betting around 40-50% of the pot, I can likely force folds from hands like low pocket pairs or middle cards that missed the flop. If I am called, I still have two overcards and potential to improve or bluff on later streets if the board remains favorable.

The flop is paired with two Jacks and a four, providing a dry, disconnected board. As I hold Ace-high, I have minimal showdown value and am effectively facing a bluff-catcher scenario against the pre-flop aggressor. Continuing here with Ace-high is suboptimal as it is highly likely I am behind any pair or better, and calling creates a bloated pot out of position with a weak range. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and prudent decision to conserve my stack.

I am in the button position with Queen-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. In a three-handed game, playing junk cards like this from early position (first to act pre-flop) is unprofitable. The best strategic decision to maintain stack health is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 6h, an uncoordinated and weak offsuit hand. Even though Flashlight folded and it's only 50 chips to limp in, playing 96o out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding is the best decision.

7h 9s is a weak offsuit one-gapper. Even though I am on the Button, this hand is not strong enough to profitably open-raise to steal the blinds. Folding to wait for a better starting hand is the standard and most prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind with T4s. The Button has folded, so it is just me and the Big Blind. While T4s is not a premium hand, in a blind-vs-blind situation, it has decent playability and flush potential. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and try to win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), which makes defending with Queen-high in a blind-versus-blind scenario a mathematically sound decision. While Queen-seven offsuit is not a premium hand, it is within the range of hands that can comfortably call in this position to see a flop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind vs blind situation. The flop of K-9-3 is generally better for my perceived range than for the Big Blind's calling range. While I only have a backdoor diamond draw, a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard play here to put pressure on my opponent and take down the pot immediately if they missed the flop.

I am in the big blind with Queen-high (Qh 7c) on a board of 9d 3h Kh. My opponent, Flash, has made a continuation bet into me after raising pre-flop. While I have a backdoor flush draw with the Queen of Hearts, my hand has very little showdown value against a range that includes Kings or Nines. The pot odds are 3:1, but without a pair or a strong draw, calling to chase a backdoor flush on a dry board is not a profitable long-term strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qd 5h. While this is a weak hand, it is a standard spot to consider a steal attempt in a 3-handed game. However, looking at the previous hands, Flashlight and Pro have been folding frequently to pre-flop aggression, but I have also been raising quite a lot. Given the stack sizes and the relative weakness of Q5o, I will choose to fold this hand to maintain a tighter image and wait for a better spot, especially since I've won several recent pots and don't need to force action with sub-optimal cards.

Holding 4c and Ts in the small blind is a weak hand with poor potential for improvement. Since I am out of position against the big blind, the most prudent strategy is to fold and preserve my chip stack rather than chasing a weak hand.

I hold pocket Jacks (Jd, Js), which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. Being on the button, I have a positional advantage. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is the correct strategic move to build the pot, assert control, and potentially thin the field against the Small Blind and Big Blind.

KTo is a marginal, easily dominated hand. Playing it from the Small Blind against a Button raise puts me out of position for the rest of the hand, with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Eights (8s 8h). The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 300. In a three-handed game, pocket eights are very strong against a button opening range. I will 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. A raise to 900 (3x the original raise) is a standard size to build the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or define the opponent's range.

Pocket Jacks are a premium hand that is far too strong to fold to a 3-bet from the Big Blind. While I could consider a 4-bet, calling is the more prudent professional play here to maintain pot control and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. This avoids committing too many chips against a potential range that includes overpairs like QQ, KK, or AA while still allowing me to play a strong hand profitably.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with pocket Eights on a 9-6-5 rainbow board. This is a very favorable flop for my range, as I have all the overpairs (TT, JJ, QQ, KK, AA) and potentially some strong draws or sets (99). My opponent, Flashlight, called my 3-bet pre-flop, which often includes hands like middle pairs, big suited connectors, or AQ/AK. My pocket Eights are currently an overpair to the board, though vulnerable to a Nine or a higher pocket pair. I will lead with a continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 of the pot to maintain initiative, extract value from draws or smaller pairs, and protect my hand equity. A bet of 750 into an 1850 pot is a standard sizing here.

I am holding pocket Jacks, which is a strong overpair on this low-connected board. My opponent (Flash) 3-bet pre-flop, showing strength, and continued with a bet on the flop. Given the board (5c-6s-9c), there are several draws (straight and flush). I am in position, and my hand is too strong to fold. I will call the bet to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate on the turn. Raising at this point would likely isolate me against a range that crushes me (like sets 55, 66, 99) or forces out bluffs that I want to keep in.

The turn card is a Trey of Spades (3s), which doesn't significantly change the board texture (5c-6s-9c-3s). My pocket Eights are currently second pair to the Nine on the board. Flashlight called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, suggesting they have a piece of the board (like a Nine or a middle pair) or a draw. By checking the turn, I can control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff or see a cheap showdown. My hand has showdown value but is not strong enough to bet three streets for value comfortably, especially if Flashlight has a Nine or a set.

The opponent has checked to me on a turn with several straight and flush draws available (5c-6s-9c-3s). As the pre-flop aggressor and the one who bet the flop, checking back here would lose initiative and allow the opponent to see a free river card with a draw. My pocket Jacks are currently a strong hand, and I need to bet to protect against those draws and extract value from worse hands like a 9x or a 6x. A bet of roughly half the pot (1600) keeps the pressure on while building a larger pot for a potential value bet on the river.

On this turn (3s), which is essentially a blank, I have checked to control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff from Flashlight. My pocket Eights (8s 8h) still represent a very strong bluff-catcher or thin value hand, beating all of Flashlight's possible draws (like 78 or club draws) and lower pairs (like 77). Since the pot odds are roughly 3:1 (I need to be right about 25% of the time to break even), and my check could have induced a bet from a wide range of semi-bluffs or even air, I will call to see the river. Folding here would be too exploitable given the strength of my hand in a 3-bet pot on this specific board texture.

On the river Jack of Clubs (Jc), the board is now 5c-6s-9c-3s-Jc. The Jc is an overcard to my pocket Eights and completes a potential club flush and several straight draws (though 78 already existed). Given the action, Flashlight called my 3-bet and flop continuation bet, then led the turn when I checked. My Eights have shifted from a strong hand to a marginal bluff-catcher. Betting here would likely only result in being called by better hands (a 9, a J, a flush, or a straight) and folding out worse hands. By checking, I can control the pot and evaluate Flashlight's response. If Flashlight bets, I will have to decide if they are representing a value hand or a missed straight draw like 78 (though 78 actually beats me now if they had it) or other air. Checking is the most disciplined play to reach showdown or minimize loss.

I have improved to a set of Jacks on the river. While the club flush has completed, my hand remains extremely strong, and I need to extract value from my opponent's range, which could include two-pair combinations or smaller sets. Since the opponent checked to me, checking behind would be leaving value on the table. I will place a value bet of 2500 into the 6550 pot to entice a call from weaker hands while keeping the sizing reasonable in case they are trapping with a flush.

The river card is the Jack of Clubs (Jc), which is a very unfavorable card for my pocket eights. The board now includes a possible club flush and the Jack is an overcard to my pair. Looking at the action, Flashlight called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, then took the initiative by betting on the turn and the river. This betting line is extremely strong and suggests a hand like a Nine, a Jack, a straight (78), or a flush. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.6:1, my pocket eights have been reduced to a weak bluff-catcher that loses to almost all of Flashlight's logical value range. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, I will fold and wait for a better spot.
53o is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity. As the first to act pre-flop on the Button, opening this hand would be highly unprofitable. A clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with K3s. The Button (Pro) has folded. The pot currently consists of my Small Blind (50) and the Big Blind (100). To call, I only need to put in 50 more chips. K3s is a decent hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). However, calling often invites the Big Blind to check and see a free flop, whereas raising can win the pot immediately or give me the initiative. Given that I'm out of position for the rest of the hand, a small raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard play to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the 150 already in the pot. If called, I have a hand with some post-flop playability due to being suited and having a King high.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (Jack-Three offsuit). While the pot odds for calling the 200-chip raise are decent, the hand itself is easily dominated by the Small Blind's raising range. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash is likely to lead to difficult post-flop decisions and negative expected value. It is more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger hand to defend.

I am on the button with K8o. In a 3-handed game, this hand is well above the average opening range for the button. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play.

I am in the small blind with 2h 7c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em. Flash has raised from the button, indicating a wide range, but my hand has no potential and I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand. Folding is the only logical decision to preserve my stack.

We are in the Big Blind with 4h 6h facing a 3x open from the Button. While the Button appears to be opening a very wide range, 64s is a weak suited one-gapper. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize our equity, and we don't have enough playability to justify calling a 3x raise or turning this into a 3-bet bluff. Folding is the most standard and sensible decision.

I am on the button with Queen-Three suited. While being in position is advantageous, this hand is too weak to warrant an open-raise or a call in a 3-handed game. I prefer to maintain my chip lead by playing only premium or speculative hands with more potential. Folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound decision given the strength of the hand.

T7o is a weak offsuit hand. Being out of position in the Small Blind against the Big Blind, this is a clear fold. There is no need to try and steal or play a marginal hand, especially against a capable opponent.
A7s is a strong starting hand to open from the button. I will make a standard raise of 2.5x the big blind to try and steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a suited ace.

I have Ace-King of Spades, which is a premier starting hand and a strong candidate for a 3-bet. Pro has opened from the button to 250. Since I am in the small blind and will be out of position post-flop, I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A raise to 850 is a standard 3-bet sizing in this situation to put pressure on the button and discourage the big blind from entering the pot lightly.

Q8 offsuit is a weak hand that does not defend well against a 3-bet, especially when playing out of position. A 3-bet from the small blind signifies a strong range, and calling here would put me in a difficult spot in a bloated pot. The most disciplined and profitable long-term strategy is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a 3.4x 3-bet from the small blind, A7s is a mediocre hand that is often dominated by the opponent's value 3-betting range (like AK, AQ, AJ, AT). Although I have position, calling here often leads to difficult post-flop situations, especially if I pair my Ace and face further aggression. Folding is a safe, standard play to preserve chips for better, more profitable spots.

I am on the button with 4s 3c, which is a very weak starting hand. While a button open is standard in many spots, we have recently lost a significant pot in Hand #19 and our hand strength here is nearly zero. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with 9-6 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with poor playability post-flop. Given that the button has folded, it is just me against the big blind. Calling would require putting in another 50 chips to see a flop, but there is no strategic justification to play such a weak hand out of position. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Ace-Nine suited is a strong hand for a 3-handed game, particularly from the button. I want to build a pot and exert pressure on the blinds, so I will make an opening raise to 250 to either take the pot down immediately or gain initiative for the post-flop play.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button, folding is the standard and most profitable decision to avoid playing a weak hand out of position.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qh 4h. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I have Q-high with a backdoor flush draw on a low, somewhat connected board (7-3-6). As the defender in the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. I hold Ace-high on a very coordinated low board (7-3-6). This texture significantly favors the Big Blind's calling range, which includes many small pairs, suited connectors, and straight draws. My hand has decent equity with the overcard and backdoor flush draw, but betting into a range that connects well with this board is risky. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially take a free card to hit an Ace or improve my flush draw, rather than inflating a pot with a marginal holding against a potential trap.

With Ace-high on a board of 7-3-6-3, I am in a marginal position. Flash has checked, indicating weakness or a trap. Betting into them could lead to being check-raised, forcing me to fold my equity. The board has double-paired (7s and 3s) with a 6. My hand is effectively two pair, Sevens and Threes, with a Queen kicker (7-7-3-3-Q). The board is paired twice (sevens and threes), meaning any pair in my opponent's range beats my Ace-high. Checking behind is the most standard and disciplined play to realize my showdown equity, as I beat hands like King-high or worse.
98 offsuit is a marginal holding that is slightly too weak to open-raise from the button 3-handed, especially against two capable opponents. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2o and the Small Blind has limped. Since I am already in the pot for 100 chips and the cost to see the flop is 0, I will check. Raising with such a weak disconnected offsuit hand is unnecessarily risky, and there is no reason to fold when checking is free.

I have top pair with a Jack on a dry board. Since my opponent checked to me, betting is the best way to extract value from weaker hands and gain information about their range. A bet of 100 represents a standard continuation bet size, protecting my hand while building the pot.

My hand, five of hearts and trey of diamonds, is extremely weak and offsuit. There is no strategic value in investing chips into this pot with such a substandard starting hand. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

With A2o on the Button in a 3-handed game, we have a weak Ace but the advantage of position. A standard open to 2.5x (250) is often profitable as a blind steal. If faced with a 3-bet, we can easily fold, but we can take down the blinds or play in position post-flop if called.

In this 3-handed game, 98o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Pro opened from the button for 250, and I am in the small blind. I am in the big blind against a button open, with two overcards on a low and dry flop (4d, 7d, 2c). The board is 4d-7d-2c-7s. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked back on both the flop and the turn, indicating weakness or a willingness to take a free showdown. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or hands with some equity, effectively turning my hand into a bluff. Our hand is now two pair (Sevens and Deuces) with an Ace kicker. Flashlight checking twice often indicates weakness, such as a missed draw, a weak 4, or just high cards. While we could bet for protection against draws, checking again exercises pot control with a marginal made hand, allowing us to see a free river and potentially catch our opponent bluffing missed draws on the next street.

The board has paired the seven three times, creating a quads 7-7-7-7-4-2 board. My hand (As-Qh) gives me the best possible kicker (Ace) for the quads. I effectively hold the nuts, as the only way I don't win the pot outright is if the opponent also holds an Ace, resulting in a chop. Since the opponent checked back the flop and turn, they are unlikely to have a strong hand, and there is little value in betting as they will either fold or chop. Checking is the standard play to conclude the hand with showdown value.

On the river, the third seven hits the board, giving us a full house: Sevens full of Deuces. While this sounds strong, it is actually the lowest possible full house on this board. Any opponent holding a 4 or any pocket pair (33, 55, 66, etc.) makes a higher full house and beats us. Since Flashlight has checked all three streets, they likely have high cards, a missed draw, or perhaps a weak pair that beats us. Betting here has very thin value, as worse hands (like King-high or missed draws) will fold, while better hands will call or raise. Checking behind is the safest play to get to showdown and win against their busted draws or weaker Ace-high hands.

I am on the button with pocket sixes, a solid medium-low pair. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and leverage my position. A 3x big blind raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size and is appropriate for this stack depth.

I am in the small blind holding Queen-Three offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Against an opening raise from the button, calling would be mathematically unprofitable and difficult to navigate post-flop. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack.

AQs is a premium hand that plays very well in 3-bet pots, both for value and to reduce positional disadvantage by taking the betting initiative. I am in the button position with pocket sixes (6h 6s). I originally raised to 300, and the Big Blind (Pro) has 3-bet to 1000. The stack sizes are even at approximately 80 big blinds. I am dealt 3h 2s, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. Being in position on the button, I have the option to open the pot, but with such a poor hand, it is mathematically correct to fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my chip lead.

With Jack-Ten offsuit in the small blind and the button folded, I have a decent starting hand to steal the big blind or play a heads-up pot with the initiative. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and gives me a good chance to win the pot uncontested.

I flopped trip Jacks on an Ace-high board. Since I raised pre-flop, my opponent will expect me to c-bet this board frequently, especially given the Ace. On a board of AJJ, I hold KQ, which gives me a gutshot draw to a broadway straight (needing a Ten). The Small Blind has led out with a small continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 600. I am in position, and a Ten on the turn would give me a disguised and very strong hand. If the turn does not improve my hand and the opponent continues to show strength, I can re-evaluate and likely fold.

I have trip Jacks on a J-A-J-6 board. The turn (6s) is a brick that shouldn't help my opponent unless they somehow floated with exactly pocket sixes. Since Flash called my flop bet, their range likely consists of an Ace, a flush draw, or perhaps a worse pocket pair that didn't believe the flop bet. I want to continue extracting value from Ax hands and charge any heart draws to see the river. Betting around half to two-thirds of the pot achieves this perfectly.

On a board of AJJ6, the opponent (Pro) has followed up their flop continuation bet with a larger turn bet of 600 into a 1100 pot. Chasing a gutshot against a double-barrel on this board texture is mathematically incorrect. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Q2s is a very weak hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. Although it is suited, the gap is too large and playability post-flop is poor against calls or 3-bets. Folding is the standard and safest decision to preserve the stack.

Flash is raising from the small blind, which is a wide range. My hand, 9c5c, is a suited connector and playable in the big blind against a small blind raise. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call for a 400 pot), so calling to see a flop is the standard defensive play here.

I was the pre-flop aggressor, and the flop contains a Jack, which fits well within a small blind's raising range. Following the success of a similar play in Hand #15, I will bet half the pot to take the initiative and attempt to win the hand immediately.

The board is J-6-6 rainbow, which is very dry and favors the aggressor (Flash) who has a wider range in the small blind that includes most jacks and overpairs. I am on the button with 2s 4c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With two active opponents in the blinds and no reason to believe a steal will be particularly effective or profitable with this specific hand, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 3-6 offsuit, a very weak hand. Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Investing more chips to call with this specific hand out of position is not strategically sound. I will fold to avoid further losses.

I am in the button position with 3d-2s, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability or high-card potential. Entering a pot with this range is mathematically suboptimal, regardless of position. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.
With 7s9c offsuit in the small blind, the hand is too weak to play profitably even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

9c 6c is a marginal suited two-gapper. While opening wider on the button is standard in a 3-handed game, the absence of antes reduces the profitability of stealing with hands this weak. I prefer to fold and conserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am in the small blind with 7h 2c, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Since the button folded, it is just me and the big blind. I am in the small blind with King of Hearts and Queen of Hearts, a strong premium hand. I am holding J6o in the Big Blind. Facing a Button open and a Small Blind 3-bet, this hand has very low equity and zero playability. Folding is the only viable option.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. While Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands, folding to a single 3-bet from the button is too tight. Calling allows me to play a pot in position with a hand that has decent equity against a typical 3-handed 3-betting range. The flop is extremely wet with three diamonds on the board. My hand, King-High with no diamond, has very little equity against a range that calls a 3-bet. On a monotone board of 6d 2d 7d, I hold the Ace of Diamonds (Ad), giving me the nut flush draw and one overcard. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked the flop. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn on a monotone board. I hold the Ace of diamonds, giving me the nut flush draw and an overcard. Given the board texture and the 3-bet pot, Flashlight's double check suggests either a trap or, more likely, a hand that has given up or is looking for a cheap showdown. I will take the free card.

The board has four diamonds, creating a flush. My hand, King-high with no diamonds, has zero showdown value against any diamond or even a pair. My hand is Queen-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with poor playability. Even on the button, there is no justification for playing this hand given the strength of the cards and the presence of two opponents. Folding is the most profitable long-term strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 4s, which is a very weak offsuit two-gapper. Flashlight folded on the button. There is no reason to play this hand out of position against the Big Blind, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

I am dealt 8c 2d offsuit on the Button, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It has no straight or flush potential and very poor high-card value. The standard and optimal play is to fold this hand pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 2s, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has folded. While it only costs 50 more to call, 82o is extremely difficult to play out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. To preserve my stack and focus on better spots, I will fold this hand.

I am on the button with 7d 2c, which is the worst possible hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite the positional advantage, there is no reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

My hand (Qc, 3d) is extremely weak and lacks playability. As the small blind with the button having folded, I am first to act against the big blind. Engaging in a blind battle with such a poor hand out of position is negative expected value, especially against a tough opponent like Pro. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. There is no incentive to enter this pot, even from the button, as the hand is easily dominated by the blinds' ranges. Folding is the only disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with KQs after the Button folded. This is a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind encounter. I want to raise for value and to take the initiative. A raise to 3x the big blind (300) is standard, putting pressure on the Big Blind and building the pot with a premium holding.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of fives (5d 5s). The action has folded to the Small Blind (Pro), who raised to 300. With a pocket pair in a blind-vs-blind situation, I have a strong enough hand to continue. Calling is the most standard play here to keep the pot controlled and set mine, or see if I can navigate post-flop on a favorable board.

I have flopped top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) and a backdoor flush draw on a very dry board. Against the Big Blind's calling range, my hand is almost certainly best. I will make a continuation bet to extract value from weaker Kings, middle pairs, and occasional straight draws. A sizing of around 40% of the pot is ideal here to keep his calling range wide.

I have flopped bottom set (fives) on a dry King-high board. This is an extremely strong hand. Pro has led out with a continuation bet of 250 into a 600 pot. While calling could induce further bluffs, I prefer to raise here to build a pot immediately, as Pro's range from the Small Blind includes many Kings and middle pairs that will likely call a raise. I will raise to 750 to extract value and set up a potential stack-off on later streets.

I hold top pair with a strong kicker on a very dry board in a blind-versus-blind situation. Facing a flop raise, my hand is too strong to fold, as my opponent could easily be raising with worse Kings (like KJ or KT) or making a move with bluffs and backdoor draws. Three-betting here would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate me against his strongest hands, such as sets or two pair. Calling is the best option to keep his range wide, allow him to continue bluffing, and control the pot size going to the turn.

After calling the flop raise out of position, the standard approach is to check the turn to the aggressor. The 7h on the turn brings some straight draws and a heart flush draw, but my top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) remains a solid hand. Checking allows my opponent to continue bluffing with missed draws or to value bet worse Kings, while keeping the pot manageable in case he holds a stronger hand like a set, two pair, or AK. Leading out here would mostly get called by better hands and fold out his bluffs.

I currently hold bottom set with 5d 5s on a board of Ks 8c 5h 7h. After raising the flop and being called by Pro in the Small Blind, the 7h on the turn is a relatively safe card, though it introduces some heart draws and unlikely straight possibilities. Pro checked, which is expected after calling a flop raise out of position. I need to continue building the pot to extract value from hands like Kx or strong pocket pairs that Pro likely holds. A bet of 1400 into the 2100 pot (roughly 66%) is a standard size to price out draws and set up a manageable shove on the river.

Facing a turn barrel after a flop raise in a blind-versus-blind scenario, my KQ remains a very strong bluff catcher. The 7h turn introduces a flush draw and completes some unlikely straight draws (like 96), but my opponent could still be semi-bluffing with a turned draw (e.g., a heart draw, 64 for an open-ended straight draw) or overvaluing worse Kings (such as KJ or KT). Folding top pair with the second-nut kicker would make my calling range too tight and exploitable in this positional battle. I will call to evaluate the river, keeping his bluffs and weaker value hands in the pot, while controlling the size of the pot in case he holds two pair or a set.

The river improves my hand to top two pair (Kings and Queens). However, the board now features three hearts, completing backdoor flush draws, and several straight draws (like 96 or 64) got there on the turn. Since my opponent has shown significant aggression on both the flop and the turn, checking to the aggressor is the most prudent play. Leading out would mostly get called or raised by better hands like flushes, straights, or sets, while checking allows my opponent to potentially bluff with missed straight draws or value bet worse two-pair hands.

I have a set of fives on a board that has become quite dangerous. While my hand was extremely strong on the flop and turn, the river Queen of Hearts completes a potential heart flush and also makes hands like QQ or KQ possible, both of which Pro could have called with on previous streets. Pro's check on the river could be a sign of weakness (giving up with a King) or a trap with a flush. Given that the pot is already quite large (4900) and my remaining stack is 3150, betting for value is risky because the hands that call me are likely to be flushes or higher sets. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win a significant pot against Pro's one-pair hands (like AK or KJ) while avoiding a potentially tournament-ending check-raise from a flush.

With 98o on the Button, the hand is a bit too weak and uncoordinated to justify an open-raise against two opponents. It lacks both the high card value and the suit connectivity desired for a profitable steal attempt. Folding is the standard and most solid play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit (As 7h). The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). A7o is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, well above the average holding. Historically, I have been opening for 300 in these positions to apply pressure and build a pot with a range advantage. I will continue this strategy by raising to 300.

I am in the big blind with 2-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor equity. Calling the raise from the small blind would force me to play out of position with a marginal holding against an active opponent. Folding is the most disciplined and +EV decision in this spot to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey of hearts, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I've been playing an aggressive style and successfully opening many pots. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is my standard opening size from the button, and it allows me to take control of the hand or potentially win the blinds immediately. If I am called, I have a suited Ace with good playability.

I am in the small blind with 6s-3s. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an active opponent like Flash on the button. Calling 250 chips into a 450 pot is negative expected value, and the Big Blind has yet to act. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button, Q9s is a reasonable hand to defend from the Big Blind. It has decent suited and connected potential, making it playable post-flop against a wide Button opening range. I will call to see a flop and realize my equity.

I flopped middle pair on a King-high rainbow board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard approach is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This board connects well with the Button's opening range, so I will check and evaluate their action.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw on a board containing two broadway cards (King and Queen). This board texture heavily favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since the Big Blind has checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to represent a strong hand and take down the pot or force out hands that have missed. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to maintain initiative.

With middle pair (Queens) on a K-Q-6 rainbow board, we have a pure bluff-catcher. Our hand is too strong to fold to a single half-pot continuation bet, as the pre-flop raiser will bet this board texture frequently with air, straight draws like JT, or worse pocket pairs. Raising would be an overplay that isolates us against hands that beat us (Kx, stronger Qx). Calling to keep their bluffs in and evaluate the turn is the standard and most profitable line.

The turn brings another King, making the board Ks-Qd-6h-Kc. My hand is now two pair, Kings and Queens, with a nine kicker. This remains a pure bluff-catcher. Leading out on this turn card serves no purpose, as we will only get called or raised by hands that beat us (any King, a better Queen, or occasionally full houses) and we will force out all the weaker hands and bluffs that the Button might hold (such as straight draws like JT or underpairs like JJ/TT). Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard line to keep their bluffing range wide and control the size of the pot out of position.

The turn brings a second King to the board. Having bet the flop with Ace-high and been called by the Big Blind, I am now faced with a decision after the opponent checks again. While I could continue to represent a King or a Queen with another bet, my Ace-high has a small amount of showdown value against potential straight draws like Jack-Ten. Checking back controls the size of the pot and allows me to see the river for free, avoiding a potential check-raise on a paired board where my opponent's range could contain a King or a Queen that won't fold.

On the river, the board pairs the Queen, giving me a full house (Queens full of Kings) with my Qc. The only hands that beat me are Kx (Kings full of Queens) or Quads (which are impossible unless the opponent has the exact case King or Qh). The opponent's check on the turn heavily discounts strong Kings from their range, as they would typically bet trips for value and protection. Therefore, my hand is extremely likely to be the best. I should bet for value to target their Ace-highs, pocket pairs like JJ or TT, and other bluff-catchers. A bet sizing of around 40% of the pot should accomplish this while allowing me to easily fold if they happen to raise with a trapped King.

On this board of 6h Ks Qd Kc Qs, my Ace-high (Ah 3h) constitutes the best possible non-full-house hand (KKQQA). The board is double-paired, meaning any Ace plays with the Kings and Queens on board. I lose to any King or Queen (which make a full house), but I beat all lower pocket pairs (like JJ, 1010, 99) because my Ace kicker is higher than their pair, and I chop with any other Ace. Pro's river lead of 500 into 1250 is a small bet that could represent a King or Queen, but it could also be a 'value' bet with an Ace or a bluff. Given the pot odds (calling 500 to win 1750), I only need to be right or chop about 22% of the time. Since I beat all bluffs and all pairs, and chop with all Aces, calling is the most logical and profitable decision. Raising is not advisable as I only get called by hands that beat me (full houses).

I am on the button with 8-9 offsuit. While this is not a premium hand, being in position on both opponents makes it a playable hand. A standard opening raise to 250 is a common play at this table to potentially take down the blinds or gain the initiative, and it allows me to control the action in subsequent streets.
Holding Ten-Two offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button raise. This is an extremely weak hand with no playability, so folding is the only viable option.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six offsuit. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 250, and Pro (Small Blind) has folded. The pot is 400, and it costs me 150 to call. While K6o has reasonable equity against a wide button-opening range, it plays very poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive opponent like Flashlight. Given the current tournament stage and the fact that I want to avoid high-variance, marginal spots against the chip leader while out of position, I will choose to fold this hand.

T9o is a marginal offsuit connector. While we are on the button and could attempt to steal the blinds, T9o lacks the playability of its suited counterpart and frequently runs into dominating broadway hands or faces 3-bets from the blinds. It's better to maintain a tighter opening range with offsuit connectors and save our chips for more profitable situations.

Q6s is a strong hand to open from the Small Blind when the Button has folded. Raising to 300 (3x BB) allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot as the aggressor.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 6h, facing a raise from the Small Blind (Flash). The price to call is 200 into a total pot of 450, offering decent pot odds of 2.25:1. While 76o is a marginal holding, defending the Big Blind against a Small Blind open is standard practice to prevent being exploited by aggressive blind steals. I will call to see a flop.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen. The board is relatively dry, though it contains some straight draws. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should lead out with a continuation bet to extract value from middle pairs, draws, or weaker hands that Flashlight might have called with from the Big Blind. A bet of half the pot is standard here to protect my hand and build the pot.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 7-6 on a 4-Q-5 board, giving me 8 outs to improve on the turn. The pot size is 900, and I need to call 300, which offers pot odds of 3:1 (25%). With implied odds to win a larger pot if I hit my straight, calling is the most profitable play. Folding would be too weak, and raising is unnecessary since I don't have a made hand yet.

The King of Hearts on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Queens. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, checking now is a standard pot-control move. The King hits some of the Big Blind's floating range, and I am also losing to any Queen with a better kicker (which is most of them). By checking, I can see how Flashlight reacts to the scare card and potentially get to a cheaper showdown or catch a bluff on the river.

I hold 7d 6h on a board of 4s Qh 5c Kh. I have an open-ended straight draw, needing a 3 or 8 to complete my straight (8 outs). My opponent, Flash, checked to me on the turn. Checking behind is the optimal play here to realize my equity for free, as it minimizes risk while still giving me the chance to hit my straight on the river. Betting would only likely be called by hands that currently have me beat, and I have no reason to bluff against a player who might be checking to trap.

On the river, the board is 4s-Qh-5c-Kh-9d. I have second pair with a weak kicker (Queens, 6 kicker). The turn check-check suggests that Flashlight likely doesn't have a King, or is playing it very cautiously. However, my 6 kicker makes my hand vulnerable to any other Queen. By checking, I am looking to get to a cheap showdown. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate the bet size, but checking is the best way to control the pot given the board texture and my relative hand strength.

I hold 7d 6h on a board of 4s Qh 5c Kh 9d. I have no pair and no showdown value, as even a pair of fours or fives beats me. My opponent, Flash, has checked the river, signaling they likely have a marginal hand or are giving up. Since I cannot win by calling a bet (if I were to bet and get raised) and my opponent is unlikely to fold a hand that beats 7-high, checking behind is the only rational move. There is no profitable bluff here against an opponent who could easily have a Queen or King.

I am on the Button with 3h 6c, which is a very weak starting hand. Although the button allows for wider opening ranges, 63 offsuit lacks any significant equity or playability post-flop. Given the stack sizes are relatively even and I want to conserve chips for better opportunities, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 8c, which is a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Given the lack of playable equity in my hand, the most disciplined action is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with King-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand that lacks potential for post-flop value, especially in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy, and there is no reason to waste chips with a marginal holding. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

With King-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folded, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise rather than just limping. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) puts pressure on the Big Blind, giving me a good chance to take down the dead money immediately or play a slightly bloated pot with a hand that has good high-card value.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a 3x open. Since Flashlight folded, this is a blind-vs-blind battle. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. J9o is a standard defending hand in this spot, especially given the positional advantage. I will call to see the flop.

The flop is very dry and paired (4h 4c 7d). Although I completely missed the board with my King-high, this texture strongly favors my pre-flop raising range, which includes all the overpairs. A small continuation bet puts pressure on the Big Blind's missed overcards and random junk, likely winning the pot right here without needing to risk many chips.

On a dry, paired board of 4h-4c-7d, the Small Blind's continuation bet of 250 into 600 is a standard move with much of their range. I am holding Jack-high with two overcards. Given that I am in position and getting attractive pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 1100), folding is too conservative. I will call to see how the action develops on the turn. If the Small Blind checks the turn, I may have an opportunity to take the pot, and hitting a Jack or a Nine on the turn could give me the best hand.

The turn card is the 9 of diamonds, which completes some straight draws and introduces a diamond flush draw, connecting well with my opponent's flop calling range. Holding only King-high with no draws, my equity is very low. Firing a second barrel here is unlikely to fold out hands like a 7, a 9, or pocket pairs that decided to call the flop. Checking and re-evaluating (which likely means giving up to a bet) is the most prudent play to conserve chips.

On the turn, the Nine of Diamonds gives me a pair of Nines. The Small Blind (Pro) has checked after betting the flop. This check could indicate a marginal hand, a draw, or a trap with a 4. Since I have improved to middle pair, I have significant showdown value. Checking behind is a solid play here to control the size of the pot and avoid being check-raised by a stronger hand like a 4 or a slow-played overpair. It also allows me to see the river for free and potentially catch a bluff or value bet on the final street if checked to again.

The river brings the Ace of diamonds, completing the flush draw and putting a scary overcard on the board. My King-high has almost no showdown value against the range that called the flop. While the King of diamonds acts as a decent blocker for bluffing, firing a bet now after checking the turn might look inconsistent and could easily run into a hand that rivered a flush, or a floating Ace. Checking and surrendering to any bet is the safest and most disciplined choice here to conserve my stack.

On the river, an Ace has appeared. I have a pair of Nines, which has decent showdown value in a blind-vs-blind situation. Pro has checked the turn and the river. While it's possible Pro is trapping with a 4 or an Ace, it's more likely they have a marginal hand or missed cards. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by a worse hand, and I don't want to get check-raised. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and potentially win the pot with my middle pair without further risk.

With 9h Th on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop. I'll open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to build a pot and potentially steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a raise to 250. In a three-handed game, A7o is a solid hand to defend with, though it is not strong enough to warrant a three-bet for value here, especially out of position. Calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot manageable while playing against a likely wide button-opening range. I'm getting decent odds (200 to win a pot of 400) and will have the opportunity to evaluate the board strength post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 7h. The pot has been raised to 250 by the Button, and the Small Blind has called. The pot odds are excellent (150 to call into a total pot that will be 750), making this a clear defense. Kh 7h is a suited hand with decent playability, allowing me to see a flop cheaply.

I have Ace-high on a dry board of 2-3-10. While Ace-high has some showdown value in a three-way pot, I am out of position against both the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. The standard play in a multi-way pot when missing the flop is to check and see how the other players react to the board.

The flop is 2s 3c Ts. I hold Kh 7h, which has very little equity against a range that includes pairs or overcards. With no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw, betting is not viable. Checking is the standard play to see if the action remains passive or to fold if the button bets significantly.

With top pair (Ten) and a decent kicker on a board with flush and straight draws (two spades, 2-3), my hand is likely the best right now after both opponents checked. I should bet to protect my equity against overcards and various draws, and to extract value from worse pairs or drawing hands. A bet of roughly half the pot should accomplish both goals effectively.

I am facing a continuation bet of 350 from the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) in a three-way pot. My current hand is Ace-high with a Seven. While I have two overcards to the board, a Ten is a very common part of a Button opening range, and I am out of position against Pro. Furthermore, Flashlight is still left to act behind me, which increases the risk of being raised or facing further action. Calling here with just Ace-high and no significant draw in a multi-way pot is generally a losing play. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I hold King-high with no draw on a dry board (2s 3c Ts). Even though I have pot odds of roughly 3:1, I have very little equity against the button's betting range. Folding is the disciplined choice here as I have no pair and no meaningful outs to improve.
I am on the button with Q5s. In a 3-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to take control of the pot and utilize my positional advantage. A 3x raise is a standard opening size.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, this is a standard calling range against a button open to avoid being walked over. Raising (3-betting) this hand is borderline aggressive, so calling is the more prudent way to see a flop and evaluate the board texture, keeping the pot manageable while playing against the button.

K5 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially when facing a 3x open from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. While the pot odds might seem reasonable, the hand has terrible post-flop playability and significant reverse implied odds if we hit a King. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker on a board that contains a King, which is an overcard to my pair. The board texture is somewhat coordinated with potential draws (clubs). As the Small Blind, checking is the standard play here to allow the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) to continue their aggression or check back, maintaining control over the pot size while I evaluate my showdown value.

I have Queen-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw on a board (Td-Kc-8c) that is quite coordinated and likely hits the Small Blind's calling range. Since I am the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to, I have the option to take a free card. Betting into this texture with only Queen-high and limited equity is risky, as I could easily face a check-raise or a call from many hands like T-x, K-x, or club draws. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and potentially improve on the turn without bloating the pot with a weak hand.

The board has become very connected with the addition of the Jack on the turn, completing several straight possibilities (such as 9-x). I have a pair of Tens and a gutshot straight draw with the Queen. Given the board texture and my position out of the button, checking is the most prudent move. Betting would likely only get called by better hands or force me to fold to a raise. Checking keeps the pot size under control, allows me to re-evaluate on the river, and maintains my current showdown value.

I have picked up a significant amount of equity on the turn with a flush draw and a double-belly-buster straight draw (any Nine or Ace completes a straight). Given the highly coordinated board (Td-Kc-8c-Jd), it is likely that the Small Blind has at least a piece of this board or a draw themselves. Since Flashlight has checked twice, I have the opportunity to take a free card and realize my equity. Betting here might induce a check-raise from a strong hand like a straight or two-pair, which would be difficult to play against despite my outs. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially hit one of my 13 outs while keeping the pot manageable.

The river card (4h) is a blank and does not improve my hand. I hold a pair of Tens on a board that contains a King, a Jack, and an Eight, making my showdown value marginal. Since both the opponent and I have checked down the flop and turn, the pot is small and it's unlikely either of us has a monster hand. Betting would likely only be called by better hands, so checking is the safest way to take my marginal hand to showdown.
