I have Queen-high on a highly coordinated board (Td-Kc-8c-Jd-4h). I checked the flop and turn to control the pot and realize my equity, but the river was a complete brick for my hand. Flashlight has checked three times, which typically indicates weakness, but this specific board texture hits a large portion of a pre-flop calling range (any K, J, T, 8, or 9). Given the lack of a significant scare card on the river and the fact that my own line (check-check) looks equally weak, it is unlikely a bluff will force a fold from even a weak pair. I will check back and concede the pot unless Flashlight happens to have a lower missed draw like 7-6 of clubs.
6c 9s is a weak, unsuited hand with poor equity. There is no strategic value in playing this hand from the button when I can simply fold and wait for higher-equity opportunities to preserve my stack.
With 7-5 offsuit in the small blind, the hand is too weak to play, even in a blind vs blind scenario. The best option is to fold and conserve chips.

KQ offsuit is a very strong hand to open with from the Button. A standard raise to 2.5x or 3x the big blind will thin the field, build the pot, and allow us to take the betting lead going into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Hearts and Six of Hearts (A6s). The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. This is a standard opening range for the Button. A6s is a strong hand three-handed, especially given the suited-ace value. Calling is an option to keep the pot smaller, but a 3-bet allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. However, since the Big Blind (Flashlight) is still to act and has a large stack, and I am out of position against Pro if I call, I will put in a raise to 850 to define the hand. This puts pressure on the Button's wide opening range and discourages the Big Blind from entering light.

Flash (SB) has 3-bet into the Button's open and me (BB). With pocket 8s, I have a strong holding that plays well post-flop. 4-betting here would risk inflating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially strong 3-betting range from the SB. Calling allows me to see a flop, potentially set-mine, or navigate the post-flop action with a solid pair, keeping the pot manageable.

I opened KQo from the Button, which is standard. However, the Small Blind 3-bet to 850, and the Big Blind cold-called the 3-bet. A cold-call of a 3-bet from the Big Blind is extremely strong, often indicating a premium pair or big Ace. The SB's 3-bet range is also very strong. KQo is heavily dominated by both of their ranges (AQ, AK, QQ, KK, AA). I have reverse implied odds and poor equity multi-way in this scenario, so the only viable action is to fold.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet from the Small Blind. The flop comes Js 3s 7d, which I have missed entirely with my Ah 6h. Flashlight called my 3-bet from the Big Blind, which usually indicates a range of medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, or broadway cards. Given that Flashlight is in position and the board is relatively connected with a flush draw, a continuation bet might be risky against a range that likely connects with a Jack or contains a pair. I will check to see how Flashlight reacts and to control the pot size, as I currently only have Ace-high with no significant draws.

I am in position against the Small Blind who 3-bet pre-flop and checked the flop. With pocket 8s, I have a mid-pair that has some showdown value, but it is currently behind any Jack or overpair in the opponent's range. Betting here likely only gets called by better hands or folds out worse hands that have 6 outs against me. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep the opponent's range wide, potentially inducing a bluff or getting to a cheap showdown if the turn is safe.

On the turn Ten of Hearts (Th), the board is Js 3s 7d Th. My Ah 6h remains Ace-high with no draws. Flashlight called my 3-bet pre-flop from the Big Blind and checked back on the flop. This check-back on the flop could indicate a marginal hand or a draw. The Ten on the turn is a semi-connected card that could have improved a range including 89s or pocket tens. Given I am out of position and have no real equity to improve, I will check again. Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands like a Jack or a medium pair, and I'd rather try to get to a cheap showdown or fold if Flashlight shows strength.

The opponent (Flash) has checked twice after 3-betting pre-flop. This indicates they either missed the board entirely or are playing a very cautious line. My pocket 8s have reasonable showdown value against their potential air/bluffs, but they are vulnerable to overcards on the river. Betting here likely forces out hands that I am currently beating (like A-high or smaller pairs) and gets called by hands that have me beat (any Jack, Ten, or overpair). Checking allows me to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown, while keeping their bluffs in the pot.

I have Ace-high (Ah 6h) on a board of Js 3s 7d Th Tc. I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, but I checked the flop and turn, as did my opponent, Flashlight. Flashlight's checks on the flop and turn suggest they have a marginal showdown hand (like a small pocket pair or a 7) or a missed draw (like KQs or spades). While my Ace-high has very little showdown value, betting now as a bluff is risky because Flashlight's line indicates they are likely looking to get to a cheap showdown with a hand that beats mine. If I check, I may occasionally win against missed spade draws or hands like KQ/AQ that I happen to beat, and I avoid losing more chips if they have a small pair they aren't willing to fold. I will check and likely fold to a significant bet.

With pocket eights on a board of Js-3s-7d-Th-Tc, I have a mid-pair that has showdown value but is extremely vulnerable. Since the opponent (Flash) has checked through every street after a pre-flop 3-bet, it is highly likely they either have a missed draw or are also weak. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands (like any Jack or Ten, or a higher pocket pair that was slow-playing) and would fold out hands I currently beat (like A-high or 9-high). Checking is the optimal way to reach a showdown and realize my equity.

I am on the button with 9h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. While I have been active in previous hands, opening with 9-4 offsuit is too wide and likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations or losing chips to a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

The button has folded, leaving me in the small blind against the big blind. With King-Eight offsuit, I have a decent hand for a heads-up situation, but I am out of position. Rather than playing aggressively, completing the small blind to see the flop for a minimum cost is the most prudent and standard approach here.

I hold pocket tens in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. The Small Blind has just limped, which usually indicates a marginal or speculative hand, though it can occasionally be a trap. To extract value, build the pot, and protect my equity, raising is the best play here. A sizing of 3.5x the big blind (350 chips) is a standard isolation raise.

I am in the small blind with K8o, which is a weak hand to play against a raise from the big blind. I have already committed 50 chips to the pot, but calling an additional 300 chips out of position with a dominated hand is not mathematically sound. Given the aggression from the big blind, the most prudent decision is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Queen-Six offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard position to open with a raise to steal the blinds and maintain initiative. Given the stack sizes and the 3-handed dynamic, playing aggressively in position is generally profitable. I will open to 300, which has been a consistent sizing for me to effectively probe or steal the blinds.
I am in the Small Blind with 93 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 300. This is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five of clubs. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. With the Small Blind folding, I am closing the pre-flop action. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me pot odds of roughly 2.25 to 1. While Ten-Five suited is a marginal hand, defending the Big Blind against a Button open with suited cards is generally standard in a 3-handed game to prevent the Button from stealing too frequently. I will call and see a flop.

I am the first to act on the flop as the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the Button. The board is King-high (Kd-7c-6h), which significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range. I have 10-high with a backdoor flush draw and very little showdown value. The standard play is to check to the aggressor and see how they react. I will check and likely fold to a standard-sized continuation bet.

I have bottom pair on a K-7-6 board. Since the King is a card that fits well into the Big Blind's calling range, a continuation bet would likely only get called by better hands or induce a raise. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot size manageable, maintain pot control, and potentially realize my equity with the pair of 6s without bloating the pot against a likely King.

The turn pairs the King, which was already a scare card on the flop. Since Flashlight checked back the flop, it is highly unlikely they hold a King. However, my Ten-high has almost no showdown value and very little equity to improve. Leading out here as a bluff might represent a King, but it also risks getting looked down by pocket pairs or Ace-high hands that Flashlight is choosing to play slowly. I will check to see if Flashlight checks back again, allowing me a free look at the river, or if they bet, in which case I will likely fold.

With a pair of sixes on a board of 7c 6h Kd Kh, my hand has minimal showdown value and is significantly behind any king or seven. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control, avoid bloating the pot against potentially stronger hands, and reach the showdown as cheaply as possible.

The board has double-paired with Kings and Sixes (KK667). My Ten-high has almost no showdown value, as any Ace, Queen, or Jack in the opponent's Button-opening range beats me. Flashlight checked back both the flop and the turn, indicating a marginal hand or a missed draw. While a lead-out bluff could potentially represent a Seven or a slow-played King, the pot is relatively small and Flashlight might easily call with any Ace-high on this board. I will check and hope to see a check-back, though I recognize that I am likely behind.

The river 6d gave me three-of-a-kind (sixes), which combined with the pair of Kings on the board creates a full house (Sixes full of Kings). While this is a strong hand, it is still vulnerable to any King held by the opponent, which would make Kings full of Sixes. Checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown value, avoid falling into a check-raise trap, and safely collect the pot against any weaker holdings or bluffs.
J6 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even though I am on the button, there is no reason to put chips into the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce suited. While suited, this is a very weak hand to play out of position against Flashlight, who has shown a tendency to call pre-flop raises or even 3-bet from the blinds. Since the Button folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. Raising with such a weak hand into a sticky opponent is risky, and limping often invites a raise that I would have to fold to. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
I have Ad Jd on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I should raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five suited, facing a raise from the Button. While suited broadway-adjacent cards can sometimes be worth a call, Q5s is too weak to defend out of position against a button open, especially given my stack lead. I prefer to keep my stack intact and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen suited, a premium hand. Facing a button raise to 3 big blinds, a 3-bet is mandatory to build the pot for value, isolate the player, and take the betting lead out of position. A standard 3-bet sizing out of position against a 3x open is around 3.5x to 4x. I will raise to 1100.

I have a premium hand (AJ suited) in a three-handed game. While the Big Blind (Pro) has 3-bet me, my hand is too strong to fold and plays very well in position. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while retaining the positional advantage for the post-flop streets. 4-betting might overplay the hand if Pro has a monster, so calling is the most balanced and effective play here.

I flopped top pair, top kicker in a 3-bet pot out of position. This is a clear value spot. The board texture has a flush draw and a few straight draws, so betting is essential for value and protection. A sizing of roughly 1/3 of the pot (around 750 chips) is ideal to extract value from weaker queens like KQ or QJ, mid-pocket pairs that might float, and flush draws, while keeping the SPR manageable for the later streets.

I have Ace-high with two overcards (Ace and Jack) on a Queen-high board. Pro's 1/3 pot continuation bet is standard for a 3-bet pot. Given the pot odds (calling 750 to win a total pot of 3750, or roughly 5:1 odds) and my positional advantage, folding here would be too tight. I have sufficient equity against his range to see a turn card, especially since an Ace or a Jack could give me the best hand.

The 5 of spades is a terrible turn card for my hand. It completes the obvious flush draw and puts four cards to a straight on the board. Holding no spade, my top pair, top kicker is now reduced to a marginal bluff-catcher. If I bet, I will primarily isolate myself against better hands like flushes, straights, or opponents drawing with a high spade. Checking for pot control is the optimal play to evaluate my opponent's action and attempt to reach a cheap showdown.

After Pro 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, his check on the turn 5s—which completes the spade flush—suggests he is either pot-controlling a marginal hand like a Queen or an overpair without a spade, or giving up with air. My Ace-high has some showdown value and outs (Aces and Jacks). Checking back allows me to see a free river card, control the pot size, and potentially realize my equity without the risk of being check-raised.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board. Holding no spades, my top pair top kicker is significantly downgraded in value. Any single spade in my opponent's hand makes a flush, meaning a bet here would only be called by better hands (any flush) while causing worse hands to fold. Therefore, I have no value bet. Checking is the only viable option to control the pot, avoid value-owning myself, and hopefully reach a free showdown.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board (Qs 4s 3h 5s 8s). My hand, Ad Jd, has no spades and currently only Ace-high. Pro 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, but has checked both the turn and the river. In a 3-bet pot, a significant portion of Pro's range (AK, AQ, AJ, ATs, and high pocket pairs) contains a spade, which would now be a flush. While Pro's double-check might suggest weakness or a non-spade hand like AA or KK, bluffing into a range that so frequently contains a spade on a four-flush board is mathematically risky. Since I have no showdown value but would likely be called by any spade in Pro's hand, checking back is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.
83s is a weak, non-playable hand even in a three-handed environment. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve my stack.

With Q9o in the small blind and the button folded, this is a prime opportunity to apply pressure in a blind-versus-blind situation. Q9o is ahead of many hands in the big blind's random distribution. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially winning the pot right now, or setting up a continuation bet if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I have position on the Small Blind post-flop and am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, J7o is a weak hand that does not play well against a standard SB opening range. Given my current stack size is the shortest at the table, I should avoid defending with marginal hands and wait for better spots to commit chips.

Q9 offsuit is a marginal hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or called by hands that dominate me (like KQ, AQ, QJ, QT). Folding is a solid, low-variance play.

In a blind versus blind situation after the button has folded, Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) is a hand that is ahead of the Big Blind's random range. Raising is the standard play here to seize the initiative, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on Flashlight and potentially win the blinds immediately.

Holding Ace-Seven offsuit in the big blind against a small blind raise is a standard defense. With the stack advantage and playing in position, calling to see a flop is the most strategically sound approach. I have enough equity to contest the pot, and calling allows me to control the pot size while keeping my range wide against the small blind's opening range.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Deuces) on a fairly dry board. This is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle. I want to bet for value against any Ace in Flashlight's range and to protect against potential draws (like clubs or a gutshot). A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot while not driving away middle-strength hands.

I have top pair with a decent kicker (As7c) on a board of 3c-2d-Ac. My opponent, the Small Blind, has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. This board is relatively dry, although a flush draw is present. Given that I have top pair, folding is incorrect. Raising might bloat the pot against hands that beat me (like AK or AJ) or fold out air. Calling is the most disciplined play to control the pot size and keep my opponent's bluffs in, allowing me to re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card (2s) has significantly improved my hand to a full house (Deuces full of Aces). This is effectively the nuts in this situation, as it's highly improbable Flashlight holds pocket threes for a better full house. I need to continue building the pot and extracting value from any Ace or draw in the opponent's range. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (roughly 2/3 pot) is a strong value sizing that remains call-able by weaker hands like a single Ace or a flush draw.

I have top pair on a very dry and paired board (A-2-2-3). My opponent's turn bet is likely either a continuation of a bluff or a value bet with a weaker Ace or a small pair. Folding is not an option given the strength of my hand relative to the board. Raising risks bloatng the pot against a potential boat, so calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and keep my opponent's bluffs in the pot until the river.

On the river, the board is Ac-2d-2s-3c-6c and I hold Ad-2c, giving me a full house (Deuces full of Aces). This is an extremely strong hand, only losing to unlikely holdings like pocket threes (33), pocket sixes (66), or pocket aces (AA). Given the action, Flashlight likely has a range consisting of Ax hands, pocket pairs, or flushes (since the river completed the club draw). Since I have been the aggressor, I want to continue betting to extract value. A bet of 1800 into the 2800 pot is a strong value bet that can be called by an Ace or a flush, while still leaving me enough chips to navigate if the situation were different. I am essentially playing for my whole stack if raised, but 1800 is a good sizing to get called by second-best hands.

I hold top pair with an Ace and a 7 kicker. The board (A-2-2-3-6) is dry and unpaired except for the deuces. My opponent has bet on every street, suggesting they likely have an Ace, a deuce, or are attempting to bluff with a missed draw. Given the pot odds, I am getting roughly 3:1 to call. My hand is strong enough to call as a bluff catcher, as there are many combinations of weaker Aces or bluffs that my opponent could be betting here. Folding top pair would be too passive, and raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

I am on the button with 5c 2d, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button in a three-handed game allows for a wider opening range, 52o is well below the threshold for a profitable raise. Given that both opponents have shown a willingness to defend their blinds or re-raise in previous hands, there is no reason to attempt a steal with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (7-2 offsuit). Flash has folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). With such a marginal hand, completing the blind or raising would be a negative expected value move. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack.
Pocket eights are a strong hand on the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play the hand in a favorable position against the blinds.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, KTo is too weak to call and too marginal to 3-bet bluff given the positional disadvantage. Folding is the standard, most mathematically sound choice to avoid being dominated out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. Flashlight opened from the Button to 300, which is a common stealing position in a 3-handed game. Ace-Ten is a very strong hand in this configuration, well ahead of a typical Button opening range. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller out of position, 3-betting for value and to take the initiative is generally preferred to discourage the Button from realizing equity with weaker hands and to build a pot with a hand that has high equity. I will raise to 900 total.

Pocket eights are a strong hand, especially on the button. Since I am facing a 3-bet from the big blind, calling allows me to see a flop in position with a solid hand while keeping the pot size under control. 4-betting here might be too risky given the stack depths and could force me to commit too much if my opponent has a premium hand. I have good implied odds to set-mine or hit a favorable flop.

In this 3-bet pot, the flop of 5d-Kc-4d favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I have many strong Kings (AK, KQs) and overpairs (AA) in my range. Holding Ace-high with the Ten of diamonds provides me with some backdoor equity and a diamond blocker. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately one-third of the pot (650 chips). This sizing is designed to put pressure on underpairs and non-improving Broadway hands while keeping the pot manageable if I am called by a better hand like a King.

I am facing a bet of 650 into a pot of roughly 1900 on a board of Kc 5d 4d. My pocket eights are a strong bluff-catcher in this spot. The bet sizing is relatively small (approx 33% pot), which often suggests a continuation bet with air or a weak king. Folding would be too conservative given the board texture, and raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me or fold out hands I currently dominate. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and re-evaluate on the turn while maintaining my range.

The Turn card is the Ten of spades, which improves my hand to second pair with an Ace kicker. While I have improved, the board still features a King, which is a significant part of the Button's calling range in a 3-bet pot. If I bet again, I am unlikely to get better hands to fold (like a King) and might only get called by better hands or draws. By checking, I control the pot size and can comfortably call a bet from Flashlight to see a River, as my hand now has significant showdown value. This also allows Flashlight to continue bluffing with diamond draws or lower pocket pairs.

The opponent, who initiated the action with a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, has checked the turn. Checking back with pocket eights is the optimal move here to control the pot size and reach showdown. Betting would likely force out hands I already beat and invite a check-raise from hands that are stronger, while checking back allows me to see the river for free and evaluate my showdown value.

On the river, the board has paired the Ten, giving me trip Tens with an Ace kicker. This is a very strong hand, especially after the turn went check-check. Flashlight's check on the turn suggests they likely have a marginal made hand like a King or a middle pocket pair, or perhaps a missed draw. Given that the board has paired my Ten, it is highly likely I have the best hand. I will bet for value, aiming to get called by any King (like K-Q or K-J) or potentially a stubborn pocket pair like Queens or Jacks. A bet of 1800 (about 57% of the pot) is a standard value sizing that is likely to be called by a range of second-best hands.

The opponent's river bet of 1800, after checking the turn, polarizes their range to either a strong value hand (like a Ten or a King) or a missed draw turned into a bluff. Pocket eights on a board with two broadway cards (K, T) and a paired board are a weak bluff-catcher at best. Given the opponent's aggressive playstyle and the likelihood that they have at least a King or a Ten in their range, calling is not profitable. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.
I am on the Button with a very weak offsuit hand (96o). This hand has poor playability and very low equity, making it a clear fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with J7 suited. Since the Button has folded, it is a blind-vs-blind situation. J7s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise against the Big Blind to take down the pot or play in position post-flop if called. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I am in the big blind with Ten-Six offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the small blind, the pot odds of 2:1 do not justify defending with such a marginal hand out of position. It is safer to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 8s5s. In a three-handed game, opening the button is standard with a wide range of hands to apply pressure on the blinds. This suited connector-style hand has some post-flop potential if called, and I have been successful with 3x raises recently.

I am in the small blind holding suited connectors (TsJs). The button (Flash) has opened for 3x. Calling here allows me to see a flop with a decent hand and good playability in a relatively small pot, keeping the price of admission reasonable while maintaining my stack integrity.

Q9o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a standard raise from the Button and a flat call from the Small Blind, Q9o does not have enough equity or post-flop playability to justify calling the additional 200 chips out of position. 3-betting as a bluff in a multiway pot with this hand is also suboptimal. Therefore, folding is the most profitable decision.

The flop is highly disadvantageous for my J-10 high holding, as it heavily favors the button's range, which includes many Aces. I have missed the board completely and have no significant draw to justify a continuation bet or a probe. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play, allowing me to gauge the opponent's strength based on their reaction. If they bet, I can easily fold unless the price is extremely favorable or I gain significant additional equity.

The board comes Ace-high (Ad 9d 2h), which significantly favors my perceived range as the pre-flop button raiser. Flashlight checked to me, showing weakness. Even though I have nothing but 8-high, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. I will bet around 40% of the pot to represent an Ace or a strong king, putting pressure on Flashlight's mid-pairs or drawing hands.

The flop of Ace-high is extremely poor for my Jack-Ten holding, and I have missed the board entirely, holding no pair, flush draw, or straight draw. I lack the necessary equity to continue against a bet, making folding the correct, disciplined decision to protect my stack.

Q5 offsuit is a weak hand in 3-handed play, even from the button. Investing chips with such a marginal holding is not optimal, as it has poor equity against the ranges of my opponents. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit, a very weak hand. The Button folded, so it's on me. Given the poor playability of this hand and being out of position against the Big Blind, the best action is to fold.

A2o is a weak offsuit Ace. While opening from the button is a marginal option in 3-handed play due to blocker value, A2o plays very poorly post-flop and is easily dominated if called or 3-bet by the blinds. Folding is a safe, solid decision to avoid tricky situations and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 3s, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has folded. While it's tempting to try and steal the Big Blind from Flashlight, 5-3 offsuit has almost no playability post-flop if called, and Flashlight has shown a willingness to defend his blinds and call raises in previous hands. Given my current chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I am on the button with K9h. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

Holding Td-5h in the Small Blind is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and no suited potential. Given that Flash has raised from the Button and the Big Blind is still to act, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising with this trash hand. The most disciplined and profitable play in the long run is to fold and wait for a more favorable spot to defend or 3-bet.

K5o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x button raise. Even though the opponent might have a wide stealing range, playing this hand out of position will lead to tough post-flop decisions and poor reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I am in the button position with KTo, a hand that has good equity in a 3-handed game. As the first to act, I should open the pot to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and pressure the blinds.

With QJs in the Small Blind facing a Button open, 3-betting is generally the best approach. It punishes wide Button steals, neutralizes the positional disadvantage by taking the betting initiative, and denies the Big Blind a chance to squeeze or realize equity. I will 3-bet to 1000.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five of Diamonds. Flashlight (Button) opened for 300, and Pro (Small Blind) 3-bet to 1,000. This puts me in a 'sandwich' position. While A5s is a strong hand in 3-handed play, cold-calling a 3-bet from the Big Blind is generally a losing play because it leaves me vulnerable to a 4-bet from the original raiser (Flashlight) and often puts me in a bloated pot with a dominated Ace. A5s is a candidate for a 4-bet bluff, but given that Pro (the 3-better) has a significant stack and has shown aggression, I prefer to fold and preserve my chip lead rather than commit nearly 10% of my stack to a high-variance play out of position against one or both opponents.

Pro (SB) has 3-bet my open to 1000, and I am sitting on the button with King-Ten offsuit. KTo is a hand that plays reasonably well in position, and the pot odds (calling 700 to win a total pot of 2100, including the blinds and the 3-bet) justify a call. 4-betting here would be overplaying a marginal hand, and folding would be too tight given I have position and a reasonable holding. I will call the 700 to see a flop.

On this A-high board in a 3-bet pot, my range has a significant range and nut advantage, as I have all the strong Aces (AA, AK, AQ) while my opponent's range is mostly capped at marginal Aces, medium pocket pairs, and some suited broadways/connectors. Although I completely missed the flop with Queen-Jack high, a small continuation bet will fold out many of my opponent's floating hands and underpairs (like 99-QQ) that currently beat me. I will size it around 1/3 pot.

I am facing a continuation bet from an opponent who 3-bet pre-flop on an Ace-high board. My holding (King-high) has absolutely no showdown value or draw potential. Given the range disadvantage and lack of equity, calling would be mathematically unsound. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.
I am on the button with 2d 4c, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. This is an easy fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 7d. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While 9-7 offsuit is a speculative hand that could be used for a steal or a completion in a 3-handed game, Flashlight has shown a tendency to call pre-flop raises frequently. Given that I am out of position and the hand is relatively weak, I will choose to fold to avoid playing a potentially difficult pot against a player who defends their blind often.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While I have been playing aggressively in previous hands to maintain pressure, J4o is significantly below the standard opening range for the button in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 120 big blinds, and there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a hand that has poor playability and is easily dominated. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With Ten-Deuce suited in the small blind and the button having folded, the hand is too weak to justify completing the big blind against a tough opponent like Pro. I will fold to conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My position gives me the advantage, and raising is the standard play to take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds. I will raise to 250 to set a standard opening size.

6d 9s is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Facing a raise from the button, this is an easy fold from the small blind. Playing this hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser would be unprofitable in the long run.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight opened from the Button with a standard raise. To capitalize on my hand strength and take control of the pot, I will put in a 3-bet. This either wins the pot immediately or allows me to play as the aggressor post-flop. Given the stack sizes, a raise to 850 is a standard sizing.

I opened with Queen-Jack offsuit on the button, which is standard. Flash has 3-bet from the big blind. While QJo is a marginal hand against a strong 3-bet range, I have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds are decent enough to justify a call to see a flop, as folding would surrender the initiative too easily. I will call the 600 additional chips to see the flop in position.

On a paired board like Q-Q-4, the 3-better (me) typically maintains a range advantage. I have many overpairs (AA, KK, JJ, TT) and AK in my range that want to extract value or protect equity. While I missed this flop with Ace-Jack, a small continuation bet of roughly 30% of the pot is effective here to fold out his air and put pressure on his mid-to-low pocket pairs. Given Flashlight's shorter stack, I want to keep the pot manageable while still exerting the pressure my pre-flop aggression warrants.

I have flopped trip Queens (Q-Q-Q) with a Jack kicker on a board of Qd-Qh-4d. This is an extremely strong hand. The opponent's lead of 550 into a pot of approximately 1700 looks like a value bet with a lower pair or an attempt to probe with an Ace-high hand. Raising now might force them to fold their bluffs or lower value hands, which I want to keep in the pot to extract further value. I will call to maintain pot control and see the turn, allowing the opponent to continue betting with a wider range.

On the turn, the Ace of Diamonds completes a possible diamond flush and also gives me Top Pair. Given the board is paired (Queens) and I was the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace is a very strong card for my range. However, because Flashlight called my flop continuation bet on a Q-Q-4 board, they could easily have a Queen or a pocket pair. Checking here allows me to control the pot size and observe Flashlight's action. If Flashlight checks back, I likely have the best hand; if they bet, I can evaluate the sizing to determine if they are representng a Queen, a flush, or a bluff.

I have flopped trip Queens with an Ace on the board. Flash checked the turn, which likely indicates he is either on a draw or testing for strength. Since I have a strong made hand, I need to bet for value and to protect against potential flush draws available on the board. A bet of 1200 into the 2850 pot is a solid sizing to extract value while maintaining control.

I have Top Pair with an Ace on a paired board. Flashlight's bet of 1,200 into a 2,850 pot is somewhat polarized. While the Ace of diamonds completes a potential flush and I lose to any Queen, I am getting roughly 3.4-to-1 pot odds. Folding Top Pair in a 3-bet pot against a button opening range is too tight, especially since the Ace likely improved my hand. I will call the 1,200 to see the river. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me, so calling is the most efficient way to reach showdown or see if Flashlight continues their story on the river.

The river 7c is a complete brick. I have top pair with an Ace kicker, but the board is coordinated with a pair of Queens and three diamonds. My check on the turn induced a bet from Flashlight, which I called. Now on the river, I will check again to see what Flashlight does. If I bet, I am likely only getting called by hands that beat me (a Queen, a flush, or perhaps a slow-played AK). By checking, I allow Flashlight to bluff with missed draws or check behind with weaker made hands, while keeping the pot manageable against his remaining stack.

I have trip Queens on a board of Qd-Qh-4d-Ad-7c. This is a very strong hand. Flash has checked the river, which makes it unlikely he has a monster hand like 44 or 77 (which would be a full house). He likely has an Ace, or a missed draw. Since I am in position and have already built a strong pot, I will bet for value, hoping to be called by an Ace or a smaller pair.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a board that is double-paired with Queens. Although there is a possibility of a flush or Flashlight holding a Queen, the pot odds are overwhelming. Flashlight is betting 1,000 into a pot of 5,250, meaning I am getting 6.25-to-1 on a call. At these odds, I only need to be right about 14% of the time to break even. Given Flashlight's wide button-opening range and the small sizing on the river, they could be betting for thin value with a weaker Ace, a pocket pair, or even bluffing with a missed diamond draw. Folding top pair with these odds in a 3-handed game would be significantly exploitable. I will call to see the showdown.

A2s is a solid starting hand on the Button in a 3-handed game. Opening with a raise allows me to take the initiative, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially win the pot uncontested. It also plays decently post-flop with nut flush and wheel straight potential.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for a raise to 2.5bb. This hand is well below the threshold for a profitable defense or a 3-bet bluff from out of position, especially with a stack of 85bb and a live player in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Defending my big blind with a pocket pair is a standard and profitable play, especially against a button open. I have good set-mining value and the pot odds are favorable. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to evaluate the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of sixes on a Queen-high board. While this is a mid-pair, it is vulnerable to overcards and draws. Betting out of position often bloats the pot with a hand that is likely only ahead of air or small draws, and checking allows me to control the size of the pot and see how the aggressor acts. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot manageable and to prevent being pushed off my equity by a continuation bet.

With the nut flush draw and a wheel gutshot (12 outs total, plus overcard outs), my hand has massive equity on this flop. Betting as a semi-bluff is the optimal play, as it applies pressure on Flashlight to fold out weak pairs or overcards, while simultaneously building the pot in case I hit my strong draw. A continuation bet of around half the pot gives a good price on the semi-bluff and keeps my sizing consistent with my overall strategy.

I am defending my Big Blind with a pocket pair of sixes on a Queen-high board. While I am behind any Queen or better, 66 is still a decent holding against the wide range of an opponent opening from the button. The board has some drawing potential with spades, but my pair is currently ahead of all non-paired undercards and high-card bluffs. A bet of 250 into a pot of 800 offers favorable odds (pot odds of ~3:1). Folding is too tight, and raising would only isolate me against hands that have me crushed or force out the air I want to keep in the pot. Calling is the most balanced and profitable action to evaluate the turn.

I am currently out of position in the Big Blind against the Button. On the turn, the board is Qh-5s-4s-7h. I hold a pair of sixes and an open-ended straight draw (any 3 or 8 provides a straight). Checking is the most balanced play here; it allows me to realize my equity for free, control the size of the pot, and avoid bloating the pot against a range that potentially includes stronger pairs or better draws. If the opponent bets, I can re-evaluate based on the size, but checking keeps my options open and prevents me from overplaying a marginal hand.

The turn card is the 7h, which doesn't improve my hand directly but leaves me with a very strong semi-bluffing combination: the nut flush draw and a gutshot to a wheel straight (12 clean outs). Flashlight's second check signals weakness or a marginal made hand. A double barrel here applies maximum pressure on mid-pairs, weak Queens, or smaller draws, generating significant fold equity. Furthermore, building the pot now ensures a substantial payout if I complete my draw on the river. Betting around 60% of the pot is a strong, polarizing size.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of sixes on a board of Qh-5s-4s-7h. I have an open-ended straight draw (any 3 or 8 gives me a straight, providing 8 outs). The pot is 1650 and I need to call 600. The odds are approximately 3:1 (27%), and with an 18% chance of hitting my straight on the river, the call is mathematically justified, especially considering the potential for my pair of sixes to be the best hand against bluffs or weaker holdings. Folding would be abandoning too much equity, and raising as a semi-bluff against a strong line is risky. Calling is the most stable path to realize my equity.

I have completed a straight (3-4-5-6-7) on the river, which is a very strong hand given the board texture. There are no pairs on the board, so no full house is possible, and no flush is possible. With the pot currently at 1950, a value bet of 800 is appropriate to get value from a Queen, a weaker pair, or a missed draw that might still call. Betting also prevents me from missing out on value if the opponent checks behind.

The river completes my A-2-3-4-5 wheel straight, but the board's 3-4-5-7 texture means any 6 makes a higher straight. Flashlight's lead out of 800 could be a blocker bet with two pair or a set, or a bluff with a missed flush draw. Raising here is too thin and risks isolating against exactly a 6. Calling is the most balanced and profitable line to beat weaker value hands and bluffs without overexposing my stack to a superior straight.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am on the button and need to build a pot. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 chips) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and will likely induce action from the blinds without immediately signaling the extreme strength of my hand. I am prepared to play a large pot or respond to any re-raises.

I am in the small blind with King-Queen suited, which is a very strong hand to play against a button raise. 3-betting is the optimal strategy here to take control of the hand, potentially isolate the opponent, and discourage the big blind from coming along cheaply. I will raise to 1000 to build the pot while in a strong position pre-flop.
With QTo in the Big Blind facing a button raise and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my hand is too weak and likely dominated by both opponents' ranges. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I hold pocket Aces, the best possible starting hand. After my initial raise, Flashlight 3-bet to 1000. This is an ideal situation to 4-bet for value. I will raise to 2500, which builds the pot and prepares for a potential shove, while keeping Flashlight's range wide enough to potentially call or move all-in with inferior hands.

Flash has 4-bet to 2500 after my 3-bet. With King-Queen suited, I have decent equity against a standard 4-bet range. Given the stack sizes (Flash has approximately 6050 total), calling to see a flop is a solid play to realize equity without overcommitting in a potentially dominated scenario, though I will be out of position post-flop.

The board texture of [Ah, Ac, 5c] is extremely disadvantageous for my King-high hand. Flash 4-bet pre-flop, which heavily weights their range towards Aces or high pocket pairs. With no pair and no draw, I have essentially zero showdown value. Betting is futile as it will only be called by better hands or raised. I will check to see how the opponent acts, keeping my options open to fold if they continue betting, given their range advantage on this board.

I have flopped quad Aces, the absolute nuts. The board of Ah-5c-Ac is extremely dry and I heavily block the opponent's continuing range (like AK or AQ). Since I was the pre-flop 4-better, my range is perceived as very strong, and betting now might force a fold from pocket pairs like KK or QQ that are currently drawing dead. By checking, I allow Flashlight to potentially bluff on the turn or catch a card that gives them enough confidence to commit more chips to the pot. There is zero risk of being outdrawn, so slow-playing is the most effective way to maximize value.

The board is extremely Ace-heavy (Ah-Ac-5c-Jc), which significantly favors the 4-betting range of the opponent, who likely holds an Ace or a high pocket pair. I am playing King-high and have zero showdown value. Since the opponent checked behind on the flop, there is a possibility they have a hand like Kings or Queens, or they are trapping with an Ace. Betting here would be a mistake, as it only gets called by better hands and accomplishes nothing. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot size under control, and potentially induces a bluff from the opponent if they are also on the weaker side of their range.

I have quad Aces, the absolute nuts. After slow-playing the flop to allow Flashlight to catch up or bluff, the Jack of Clubs on the turn is an interesting card. Since I hold all four Aces, Flashlight cannot have an Ace; their range is likely pocket pairs like KK, QQ, JJ, or perhaps high suited connectors. If Flashlight has JJ, they have just filled up (Jacks full of Aces) and will certainly commit more chips. If they have KK or QQ, they may be suspicious but might call a small-to-medium bet thinking I am trying to take the pot away after a missed flop C-bet. I will bet 1500 into the 5100 pot to build value and set up a manageable river shove, while making it look like a delayed continuation bet or a steal attempt.

The opponent (Flash) 4-bet pre-flop, signaling a premium range (likely AA, KK, QQ, or AK). The flop came Ace-Ace-5, which heavily favors the aggressor's range, even though they checked the flop. By betting the turn on a board of A-A-5-J, the opponent is showing strength or polarized intent. With only King-high and no pair or significant draw, I have zero showdown value and no realistic path to winning the pot against a 4-betting range. Calling is mathematically and strategically incorrect given the board texture and action; the most prudent decision is to fold.

Pocket sevens are a strong hand on the button. Raising to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard opening size to isolate the blinds and either take down the pot immediately or play the hand in position against the big blind or small blind.

KQs is a premium hand, particularly strong against a late-position open from the Button. 3-betting here is the optimal play to take the initiative, build a pot with a strong holding, and squeeze the Big Blind out of the hand, guaranteeing heads-up play at worst. A 3-bet sizing of roughly 3.5x to 4x (900 chips) from the Small Blind properly punishes the open while mitigating our positional disadvantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 84o, which is a very weak hand. There has been an open from the Button to 250 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 900. Given my hand strength and the significant action ahead of me, there is no profitable way to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in position on the button holding pocket sevens. Pro, the small blind, has 3-bet me to 900. With 9000 chips, I have plenty of stack depth to call the 650 supplement to see a flop. Pocket sevens have good playability in position, and while a 4-bet is possible, a call is more controlled, allowing me to evaluate the flop or potentially set-mine against Pro's likely high-card range.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a strong perceived range advantage. My hand, Kh Qh, hasn't connected directly with the board but has substantial equity with two overcards, a gutshot straight draw to the nuts, and backdoor flush potential. A continuation bet of roughly one-third of the pot (650 chips) applies optimal pressure, often folding out the opponent's marginal holdings like low pocket pairs or unpaired broadways that lack a club. This sizing maintains the initiative while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

Pro's bet of 650 into a 2550 pot is a relatively small continuation bet, offering favorable pot odds of roughly 4:1. With pocket sevens on a 9-T-3 board, I am currently behind most of Pro's 3-betting range, which likely includes overpairs or high cards like AK/AQ. However, with good position, I have the opportunity to realize my equity or take the pot away later if Pro checks the turn. Calling is the most calculated decision to see a turn card without overcommitting, as I am not ready to fold yet given the price.

The Ace on the turn is one of the best cards in the deck for my perceived pre-flop 3-betting range, hitting my strong hands like AK, AQ, and AA perfectly. My opponent's calling range on the flop is largely capped at medium pocket pairs (JJ, QQ), weak tens, or draws. Double-barreling here tells a very credible story of having an Ace and puts immense pressure on those marginal made hands, forcing them to fold. Additionally, my Kh Qh gives me a gutshot to the Broadway straight, providing four outs to the absolute nuts on the river if I do happen to get called.

With a pair of sevens on a board of 9-T-3-A, my hand is extremely vulnerable. Pro's continuation bet on the flop and now a significant bet on the turn on an Ace-high board strongly suggests they have at least an Ace or a superior pocket pair. With no significant draw to improve my hand and facing a reasonable bet size, calling would be burning chips against a likely better range. Preserving my stack for a better spot is the most prudent tournament strategy here.
Holding 63o on the button, this is an extremely weak starting hand with very little playability or equity. Folding is the only viable option pre-flop to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 8d. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) left to act. Qd 8d is a strong suited hand in a blind-on-blind scenario, comfortably within the range for an open-raise. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. My cards (Jc, 2c) are suited, offering some potential, and the pot odds (200 to win a total pot of 400) are favorable enough to justify a call to see a flop, especially considering the small blind often has a wide opening range.

I raised pre-flop and the Big Blind called. The flop is King-high (K-7-2), which typically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. While I only have Queen-high, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on the opponent's marginal hands and take down the 600-chip pot. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to represent a King or a strong pocket pair.

The flop is King-high, which heavily favors the Small Blind's opening range. My bottom pair of deuces is extremely weak and lacks significant equity against potential kings or pocket pairs. Calling a bet here puts me in a difficult position for the turn, and given the board texture, it is unlikely my pair is ahead or will improve sufficiently to win a large pot. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to exert pressure and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 8-4 offsuit. This hand has very poor playability, no connectivity, and lacks potential for high-value combinations. Calling an opening raise from the button out of position with such a weak holding would be a negative expected value decision. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With 75s in the Big Blind against a Button open, we have a hand with decent playability post-flop due to its flush and straight potential. Given the stack depth of over 100 BBs, we are getting acceptable odds to defend and see a flop heads-up.

I have flopped bottom two pair on a connected, wet board (9s 5s 7c). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the initial raiser is the standard play. This allows Flash to continuation bet with a wide range, and I can then look to check-raise for value and to protect my hand against the numerous straight and flush draws available.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding Jack-Ten offsuit. The flop of 9-5-7 with two spades provides me with a gutshot straight draw (to an 8) and an overcard. The Big Blind has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (350 chips) to fold out weaker hands like small pocket pairs or unpaired hands that missed the flop, while maintaining the initiative in the hand.

With bottom two pair on a wet, highly coordinated board featuring numerous straight and flush draws, my hand is currently strong but vulnerable. As planned, I will check-raise to charge these draws heavily and extract value from overpairs, top pairs, and combo draws. A raise to 1100 applies the right amount of pressure while building the pot.

Pro has check-raised me on a very coordinated board (9s-5s-7c). This board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which can include many combinations of two-pair, sets, or straight draws like 86. While I have a gutshot straight draw and overcards, the check-raise is a strong show of force. Calling 750 into a pot of 2100 requires about 26% equity. A gutshot only hits about 8% of the time on the turn. Even if my overcards are 'live', they are vulnerable to better hands and the flush draw. Given the tournament context and the strength shown by Pro, folding to preserve my stack is the most prudent professional play.
Jack-three offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand does not justify an open raise from the button. Folding is the standard and disciplined choice.

52s is a very weak hand, even in a blind versus blind situation. Folding is the most straightforward and mathematically sound decision here to avoid bleeding chips out of position with marginal holdings.
I am on the Button with a very weak offsuit hand, Jack-Three. This is not a playable hand from any position, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit after the Button has folded. A2o is a standard opening hand in a head-up situation against the Big Blind. I will follow my established pattern of raising to 300 to either win the blinds immediately or play the pot with the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o. Flash has raised to 300 from the Small Blind, and I have already contributed 100 as the BB. The pot is currently 400 and it costs 200 to call. Getting 2:1 on a call, it is mathematically sound to defend my big blind with a reasonable hand like Q7o, especially in a three-handed game. I will call to see a flop.

On a relatively dry board of Th-3d-8s, as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to represent a strong range and push the opponent off their air. While I only have Ace-high, a bet of 300 (half-pot) is an efficient way to take the pot down immediately against hands that missed this flop, while still maintaining some showdown value if called and the turn improves my hand or provides a better bluffing opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high and no pair or draw on a ten-high board (Th-3d-8s). The opponent, Flash, has continuation bet 300 into a 600 pot. I do not have sufficient equity to justify a call and chasing a backdoor scenario is not profitable against this aggression. I will fold and preserve my stack.

King-five suited is a standard opening hand from the button in a three-handed game. I have been consistently opening for 300 chips throughout the tournament, and I will continue with that sizing here to put pressure on the blinds while maintaining a balanced range. Being in position for the rest of the hand makes this a profitable open.

I am in the small blind with a weak, speculative hand (T3 suited). Playing out of position against a button raiser with this hand is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation where I will be dominated. As the short stack, it is crucial to preserve chips and wait for a better opportunity to contest pots, rather than calling raises with marginal cards.
I am in the Big Blind with Q2 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a button raise, this is a clear fold. There is no strategic value in defending or 3-betting with this hand.

K9o is a weak hand to open with, particularly from the button when I am the short stack (7000 chips against opponents with 11000+). Protecting my stack is a higher priority than chasing a marginal hand in this position. Folding is the most disciplined move here.

Holding A3s in the small blind after the button folds, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise. A raise to 3x (300) puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to win the pot pre-flop or play heads-up with a premium suited Ace.

I am in the Big Blind with 65o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 3x. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 65o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the skill of the opponent, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than bleed chips with marginal hands.

I am on the button with 75o, a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. With two opponents behind me, there is no reason to put chips in the pot with such poor cards. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

K6o is a relatively strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since Pro folded on the button, I have the opportunity to attack Flashlight's big blind. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on a shorter stack.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong holding in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Given the pot odds and the fact that I am in the big blind, defending is the standard and correct play to see a flop. I have sufficient stack depth to continue playing post-flop.

On a board of As-Kd-4d, I have second pair with a King. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, an Ace-high board is very favorable for the raiser's range, but also hits many hands the Big Blind defends with. By checking, I am controlling the size of the pot with a hand that has showdown value but is unlikely to get three streets of value. It also allows me to induce bluffs from missed draws or weaker hands. If Flashlight bets, I intend to call to see a turn.

I have top pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker on a board of Kd-As-4d. With the opponent checking to me, betting is the best option to extract value from weaker aces, middle pairs, or inferior holdings, while also offering some protection against potential diamond flush draws. A bet of 300, half the pot, is a standard sizing to accomplish these goals while keeping the pot manageable.

I have second pair (Kings) on an Ace-King-4 flop. While the Ace is a scare card, my hand is too strong to fold to a single half-pot bet from the Big Blind, especially since I was the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight could be betting with an Ace, a weaker King, a diamond draw, or even air to see where they stand. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the board develops on the turn without overcommitting with a medium-strength hand. I am getting 4:1 odds on the call, which makes this a standard continuation call to see a turn.

On a board of As-Kd-4d-5h, I have second pair with Kings. I checked the flop and called a bet from Flashlight to control the pot size while retaining showdown value. The 5h on the turn is a relative brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I am first to act and will check again to continue my pot-control strategy. Checking allows Flashlight to continue betting with draws or bluffs that I can potentially beat, or to check back with weaker hands, whereas betting might only get called by an Ace or a stronger King.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board of Kd-As-4d-5h. The opponent checked to me on the turn, suggesting they are either weak or checking to induce a bet. Since I have a strong hand, I should continue betting for value to get paid by kings, weaker aces, or draws. A bet of 600, which is half the pot, is a balanced size that builds the pot while not committing too much if the opponent check-raises.

On a board of As-Kd-4d-5h, I have second pair with Kings. I checked the flop and called a bet of 300 from Flashlight to control the pot size while retaining showdown value. Flashlight has now fired a second barrel of 600 into a 1200 pot. While the Ace is a significant scare card, in a blind-versus-blind battle, Flashlight's range is wide and could include flush draws, straight draws (like 2-3 or 6-7), or pure bluffs. My hand is too strong to fold to a half-pot bet given the 3:1 pot odds, as I still have significant showdown value against the non-Ace portion of Flashlight's range. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final card and Flashlight's action.

On the river, the board shows Kd-As-4d-5h-2d. I have second pair with Kings (Kh-6d). The river 2d completes a possible flush and a straight (A-2-3-4-5) if the opponent holds a 3. Given the action so far, I have been in check-call mode to control the pot size and utilize my showdown value. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, so checking is the most appropriate move. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but my primary goal is to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

I have top pair (Aces) with a solid kicker on a board that contains three diamonds. Since I hold the Ace of Diamonds, I significantly block the potential flush that the opponent could be representing, making it less likely they have the nut flush. Having been the aggressor throughout the hand, checking might look like I am giving up, and betting allows me to extract value from Kings or weaker Aces that might call. A bet of 800 into a 2400 pot is a value-bet size that is not overly committing if I am beaten by a random two-pair or a smaller flush.

On the river, the board is Kd-As-4d-5h-2d. The 2d is a significant scare card as it completes both a potential flush and a straight (if Flashlight holds a 3). Flashlight has bet 800 into a 2400 pot, which is a 1/3 pot-sized bet. This sizing often represents either a thin value bet or a cheap bluff. While my second pair (Kings) is now a bluff catcher against many parts of Flashlight's range (Aces, flushes, straits), the pot odds are 4:1, meaning I only need to be correct 20% of the time to break even. In a blind-versus-blind battle, Flashlight's range remains wide enough to include missed draws or weaker made hands turned into bluffs. I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with 6h 2c, which is a very weak starting hand. While being on the button allows for some stealing opportunities, 6-2 offsuit is near the bottom of the range and does not warrant a raise or a call. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 7s-2s. Given the strength of this starting hand and the fact that I am out of position against the Big Blind (Pro), the most prudent strategy is to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

As the button in a three-handed game, Jack-Seven offsuit is a playable hand to open with in order to steal the blinds. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is an appropriate size to put pressure on the small and big blinds.
With Q2 offsuit in the small blind facing a raise from the button, my hand is much too weak to continue. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with T5o. Flashlight raised from the Button to 250. Although the price to call is only 150 more, T5o is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability out of position. I have folded similar trash hands to Flashlight's button opens before, and there's no reason to change that strategy now with such a weak holding.
I am dealt 4d 9c on the Button. This is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability. It's an easy fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh6s. The Button (Pro) has folded. The pot is currently 150 (100 from the BB and 50 from me). It only costs 50 more to call. While Qh6s is a weak hand, in a blind-vs-blind situation, it has decent high-card value and some playability. However, raising to take the pot pre-flop or calling to see a flop cheaply are both options. Given the shallow investment needed and the risk of being re-raised if I raise, I will simply call the extra 50 to see a flop and keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

Flash (Small Blind) has limped into the pot, completing the blind. With Jack-Five offsuit, I have a relatively weak hand and no initiative to raise. The best strategic play is to check, see the flop for free, and re-evaluate based on the community cards.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker in a blind-vs-blind limped pot. My opponent checked their option pre-flop, meaning their range is very wide. On a board of Qd-3d-Jc, there are several draws (diamonds, K-T, T-9) and middle-pair hands that might call a bet. I will lead out with a bet of 150 (75% of the pot) to extract value and protect my hand from various draws while I likely have the best hand.

I hold middle pair with a Jack on a Queen-high board. Flash's bet of 150 into a 200-chip pot is small, offering favorable pot odds to continue. Given that I have a pair, folding would be too conservative, but raising does not offer much value due to my weak kicker. Calling is the most efficient way to see the turn and re-evaluate.

I currently have top pair with a weak kicker in a limped blind-vs-blind pot. The turn 5s is a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. My opponent called my flop bet, which suggests they could have a Jack, a draw (like diamonds or a straight draw), or a weaker Queen. I will bet 350 into the 500 pot to continue extracting value from those hands and to protect against draws while I likely still hold the best hand.

I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Fives) on the turn. The board is relatively dry, and while a flush draw is possible, my two pair is extremely strong against Flash's likely range of a single Queen, a weaker Jack, or a semi-bluff. By raising to 800, I can build the pot and extract value from his continuation bets while forcing him to pay a premium to chase any potential draws.

In this blind-vs-blind battle, I have top pair on a fairly coordinated board. Flashlight's turn raise is a strong move, but in a limped pot where ranges are extremely wide, I cannot fold a hand as strong as top pair for a single raise, especially given the pot odds of approximately 3.6:1 (calling 450 to win a total pot of 1650). While my 6 kicker is weak, Flashlight could be semi-bluffing with a diamond flush draw or a straight draw (like T9 or KT), or potentially overvaluing a Jack. I will call the raise and re-evaluate on the river, likely checking to see what Flashlight does next.
