I am holding Ace-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind. This is a strong hand to defend against a Button/Small Blind raise in a heads-up situation. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is a solid play that keeps the pot manageable against the chip leader and allows me to see a flop with a hand that is likely ahead of Pro's wide opening range.

On a flop of 9-T-2 with two clubs, my Ace-high has limited immediate value. I will check to the raiser to see how they react to this board, as it favors a typical opening range but doesn't explicitly hit my hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and decide based on Pro's betting behavior.

I completely missed the flop of 9d Tc 2c, and this board connects very well with the Big Blind's defending range, including pairs, straight draws, and flush draws. Since my hand has minimal equity (just backdoor draws) and no showdown value, a continuation bet is likely to be called or raised, burning chips unnecessarily. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and see a free turn card, with the intention of giving up unless I pick up significant equity.

On the turn, the board pairs the Nine. After both players checked the flop, it's likely that neither has a very strong hand. My Ace-Eight has decent showdown value against the wide range of hands Pro might raise from the Small Blind, such as overcards or smaller Ace-highs. By checking again, I am keeping the pot small and looking to reach a cheap showdown or see how Pro reacts. If Pro checks behind, my Ace-high remains a strong candidate for the best hand going into the river.

The turn pairs the board with a Nine, and I still have absolutely nothing with 7-high and no draws. While Flash has checked twice, indicating potential weakness, betting here as a complete bluff into a board that hits many floating and drawing ranges (like flush draws, straight draws, or slow-played pairs) is an unnecessary risk. Checking allows me to get to the river for free and easily fold if faced with aggression, preserving my chip lead.

I have Ace-high in a pot that has been checked on both the flop and the turn. This suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand. While the King on the river is a scare card, betting into it with Ace-high doesn't accomplish much—better hands won't fold, and worse hands won't call. I'll check to see if my Ace-high can win at showdown.

With only 7-high, I have absolutely no showdown value and cannot win if I check. The action has checked through on every street, strongly suggesting Flash is holding a very weak pair, Ace-high, or a busted draw (like missed clubs). The King on the river is an excellent scare card because it hits my pre-flop opening range perfectly (e.g., AK, KQ, KJ) which I could have checked down. A half to three-quarter pot bluff here is highly credible and should easily fold out his marginal hands and missed draws.

On a board of 9d-Tc-2c-9h-Kd, Pro has bet 350 into a pot of 500 after both players checked the flop and the turn. My Ace-high has some showdown value, but it is extremely marginal. Pro's bet on the river after checking twice often signifies either a hand that improved on the river (like a King), a thin value bet with a middle pair (like a Ten or a Deuce) that was played cautiously, or a bluff. Given the sizing (70% of the pot) and the fact that the King is a significant scare card that hits a wide range of hands Pro might have checked, Ace-high is unlikely to be ahead often enough to justify the call. I'll preserve my stack and look for better spots.
I have a strong starting hand (QsJs) on the button. Raising is the standard play to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand, especially given the positional advantage post-flop.

With Ten-Nine suited in the Big Blind facing a 3x open, I have a strong speculative hand that plays very well post-flop. The implied odds are good since the effective stack is around 80 BBs. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here to see a flop and realize equity against the opponent's opening range.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a fairly dry, disconnected board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is standard. This protects my checking range, allows my opponent to continuation-bet with their bluffs or weaker hands, and gives me the opportunity to evaluate their sizing before deciding my next action.

On a dry flop of 2s 6d 9c, I have two overcards and a backdoor spade flush draw. After being the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately, as this board likely missed most of Pro's calling range. A bet of 300 (half-pot) is an appropriate size to achieve this.

I have top pair with a backdoor flush draw. Facing a half-pot continuation bet on a dry board, calling is the standard and most profitable play. Raising would likely fold out the bluffs in Flash's range and isolate me against better hands like overpairs or stronger nines. By calling, I keep his betting range wide and can re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, an overcard to my pair of nines that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Donk betting here is unwarranted, as it isolates me against stronger hands like Ax while folding out bluffs. Checking is the standard play to pot control, keeping Flash's bluffs in his range, and allowing me to evaluate his turn action.

The Turn card is an Ace of Hearts (Ah), which is an excellent scare card to continue my aggression. As the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor, my range contains many more Aces than Pro's calling range from the Big Blind. Pro checked the turn after calling the flop, suggesting they likely have a marginal made hand like a Nine or a Six, or perhaps a small pocket pair. By betting here, I am representing the Ace and applying significant pressure on these one-pair hands. A bet of 750 into a 1200 pot (roughly 62.5% pot) is a convincing size to force a fold from anything that isn't at least an Ace or a very strong draw.

Facing a double barrel on an Ace turn, my middle pair of nines is significantly downgraded. The Ace heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, hitting many of the Ax hands that would continuation bet the flop. Without any strong redraws and only a pair of nines, my hand is reduced to a weak bluff catcher. Calling out of position here will often lead to difficult decisions on the river against further aggression. Folding is the most prudent and disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

K2o is a very weak starting hand, even from the button. While steal raises can be profitable, this hand plays extremely poorly post-flop if called and has to fold to any 3-bet. With a comfortable chip lead, I can afford to fold the bottom of my range and wait for slightly better hands to apply pressure.

Holding 52 offsuit on the button in a heads-up scenario is generally a fold. It is one of the weakest possible starting hands with very poor equity and post-flop playability. Given my stack size relative to Pro's chip lead, I will preserve my chips and wait for a more favorable opportunity rather than attempting to steal with such a low-value hand.

K8 suited is a strong and highly playable starting hand from the button. Opening to a standard 2.5x (250 chips) allows me to either steal the blinds immediately or play a raised pot in position with strong equity and flush potential.

I am in the Big Blind holding pocket Treys. Pro has raised from the Button/Small Blind to 250. In a heads-up situation, pocket pairs have significant value, though 3s are vulnerable to overcards. I have a healthy stack of nearly 90 big blinds. Calling here is the standard play to see a flop, looking to hit a set or navigate a low board, while keeping the pot manageable against a larger stack.

I have flopped a set of Threes on a board with an Ace and a Ten. This is an extremely strong hand. Since Pro was the pre-flop aggressor and this board hits a typical raising range very well, I expect them to continuation bet frequently. I will check to the raiser to induce a bet, allowing me to either slow-play by calling or build the pot with a check-raise depending on their sizing and the flow of the hand.

The Ace-high board strongly favors my pre-flop raising range. A small continuation bet is usually enough to fold out my opponent's air and marginal pairs, while keeping my risk low if they happen to hold an Ace. With King high and a backdoor flush draw, I have some equity to fall back on, but a c-bet of 200 is an effective bluff.

I have flopped a set of Threes on a board that contains an Ace and a Ten, which are cards very likely to be in Pro's pre-flop raising range. Pro's continuation bet of 200 into a 500 pot is a standard lead, and I will raise to 650 to build the pot immediately while my hand is well-disguised as potentially a draw or a lower pair. I want to maximize value from hands like AK, AQ, or AT that will likely call a raise on this street.

My opponent check-raised my continuation bet on an Ace-high board, which typically represents a strong Ace, two pair, or a set. With only King-high and a weak backdoor flush draw, I have very little equity and no reason to commit further chips to this pot. Folding is the only sensible option here to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with a strong starting hand, King-Ten of diamonds. In a heads-up situation against Pro, this is well within my opening range. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

54o is a very weak starting hand, especially out of position. Defending the Big Blind against a standard button raise with this hand is not profitable. Folding is the correct mathematical and strategic decision.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand to play, even from the button. Although I have a comfortable chip lead over my opponent, it is best to maintain that advantage and avoid unnecessary risks with sub-optimal holdings. Folding is the standard and most sensible decision here.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in heads-up play. Given that my opponent, Pro, has been aggressive and currently holds a significant chip lead, I see no reason to invest more chips into the pot with a sub-par hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to bridge the gap in our stack sizes.

Q3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially out of position post-flop if the Big Blind decides to call or raise. Given my comfortable chip lead, there's no need to play marginal hands. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While J6o is a hand that could be played in a heads-up match, especially from the button which provides positional advantage for the rest of the hand, it is still relatively weak. Given that Pro has a significant chip lead (over 2:1) and has shown a tendency to play aggressively, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to bridge the gap in stack sizes, especially since there are only 13 hands remaining in the tournament.

K9s is a very strong starting hand heads-up on the button. I will make my standard open-raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to take the initiative, apply pressure, and start building a pot with a hand that plays well post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 7c and Pro has raised to 250 from the Small Blind. Given the pot odds (I need to call 150 more into a pot of 350), this is a standard defend. While 76o is a weak hand, it has connectivity and the price is too good to fold in a heads-up situation against a 2.5x open. I will call and see a flop out of position.

On a flop of 8c 3d 2c, my hand 7c 6h has very little equity, with only back-door straight possibilities. As the defender in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react. I will check and evaluate based on Pro's action.

I have a strong semi-bluffing hand with a King-high flush draw and overcards. A continuation bet of half the pot (250) applies pressure, gives me fold equity to win the pot immediately, and starts building a pot in case I complete my draw.

On a flop of 8c 3d 2c, I have 7h 6c which is a complete miss. Pro has placed a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 500. While I have two overcards to the 3 and 2, the 8 is the highest card on the board, and my hand has virtually no equity or drawing potential (only a very weak backdoor straight). Calling here with such a weak hand out of position is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

I am on the button with J5o. In a heads-up situation, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the Big Blind. Given the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 140/150) and I am behind in chips (9,300 vs 20,550), I need to find opportunities to pick up blinds and antes. I will make a standard 3x open to 300.

With A3s in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the button, we have a very playable hand that can flop nut flush draws, wheel draws, or top pair. Given our stack depth advantage and the decent equity of a suited Ace, calling is a standard and profitable choice to see a flop. 3-betting is an option, but calling controls the pot size with a marginal made hand.

On a highly coordinated board like 8s-Td-Qc, my Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw has very little equity against the pre-flop raiser's range, which hits this board heavily (broadways, sets, straight draws). Out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor and likely fold to a continuation bet.

I have position on the opponent and a gutshot straight draw (needing a nine). The board of 8-T-Q is quite coordinated and likely hits some of the Big Blind's calling range, but since Pro checked, I will put out a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to try and take the pot down now. If called, I still have some equity to improve on later streets.

On a highly coordinated board that heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, our Ace-high with only a backdoor flush draw has very little equity. Facing a half-pot continuation bet out of position, folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

I am dealt 2s 7d, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even though I have position, there is no strategic benefit to playing this hand, as it lacks high card value, connectivity, and suitedness. Folding is the only logical choice here.

Queen-Trey offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the small blind, especially against a chip leader. With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament, I should look for higher equity spots to close the gap rather than playing a marginal hand that is easily dominated.

Q4o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. From the Small Blind, it is generally best to fold the bottom tier of offsuit hands rather than limping or raising, as they play very poorly post-flop and lack significant equity. Conserving stack and avoiding unnecessary variance is ideal given our substantial chip lead.
AQo is a premium hand in heads-up play. I am on the button and should exert pressure while building a pot with a strong range. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I am facing a min-raise to 300 from the button while holding T7o in the big blind. T7o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. The correct strategic decision is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips, preserving my stack.

With Q3o and only 6 hands remaining in the match, there's no reason to play a trash hand. I have a 2-to-1 chip lead over my opponent, so I can afford to fold weak holdings and wait for better opportunities, or simply let the match end with my lead intact.

I have a strong suited connector/one-gapper with Queen-Nine of diamonds on the button. This is a standard raising hand in a heads-up or shorthanded situation. I will make my usual open-raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand.

With King-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind against a Button open, we have a premium starting hand for heads-up play. A 3-bet is optimal to extract value, seize the initiative, and put pressure on the opponent's wider opening range. Sizing to 1000 is consistent with standard 3-bet sizing out of position.

I opened on the button with Queen-Nine of diamonds, a solid suited hand. Pro responded with a 3-bet to 1000. Given the pot odds and my position, calling is the standard play. Folding would be too tight, and 4-betting risks too much of my stack against a potentially stronger range. I will call and play the hand in position on the flop.

I flopped top pair with a very strong kicker in a 3-bet pot. The board contains a heart flush draw and some wheel straight possibilities. Betting around 1/3 to 1/2 of the pot will extract value from worse queens, middle pocket pairs, and draw-heavy hands, while protecting my equity against overcards and draws.

I have top pair with a Nine kicker on a relatively safe board of Queen-Trey-Deuce. Pro's 3-bet range pre-flop could include overpairs like Aces or Kings, or better Queens like Ace-Queen or King-Queen, but it also contains many hands I currently beat, such as Jacks, Tens, or Big Slick/Big Little (AK/AJ). A bet of 700 into a 2000 pot is a standard continuation bet. Calling is the best option here to keep the pot manageable, maintain position, and see how the turn develops without overcommitting my stack against the top of Pro's range.

The 6c on the turn is mostly a blank, though it introduces a secondary club flush draw. With top pair and a premium kicker (KQ), my hand remains very strong against Flash's pre-flop 3-bet calling range. Betting here serves to extract value from worse queens (like QJ, QT), middle pocket pairs that might stubbornly float, and various heart or club draws. A half-pot bet of around 1700 will continue to build the pot, deny free equity to drawing hands, and set up a comfortable stack-to-pot ratio for a potential river jam.

I have top pair with a Nine kicker on a relatively dry board. Pro is showing strength by double-barreling in a 3-bet pot, but my hand is too strong to fold for a half-pot bet (1700 into 3400). Pro's range could include better Queens (AQ, KQ) or overpairs (AA, KK), but it also contains hands I beat like JJ, TT, or air like AK/AJ that is continuing the aggression. Calling is the best move to keep the pot size controlled while staying in the hand with a strong pair, as raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me.

The river is the Ten of Diamonds, which means both the heart and club flush draws have missed, as well as most straight draws. We hold top pair with a premium King kicker. Flash's calling range on the flop and turn contains worse Queens (like QJ), middle pocket pairs (like JJ or 99), and numerous busted draws. With Flash having 6,450 behind and the pot at 6,800, the stack-to-pot ratio is just under 1. Shoving all-in puts maximum pressure on Flash's marginal made hands and extracts maximum value from worse top pairs that will feel pot-committed to call. While checking to induce a bluff from busted draws is an option, a shove guarantees we get full value from the sticky made hands in their range.

On the river, the board is 2c 3h Qh 6c Td. I have top pair with a Nine kicker (Qd 9d). The action has been heavy: Pro 3-bet pre-flop, followed by a continuation bet on the flop, a barrel on the turn, and now an all-in shove on the river. In a 3-bet pot, my hand (Q9) is essentially a bluff-catcher. Pro's value range here includes better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ), overpairs (AA, KK), and potentially sets (TT, 66, 33, 22). While Pro could be bluffing with missed draws like AK or AJ, the triple-barrel shove is extremely strong. Given that this is hand 146 of 150, calling off my entire stack with a marginal top pair against a range that dominates me is too risky. I will fold and preserve my remaining 6,450 chips for the final few hands.

With 65o on the button, I have a very marginal and weak hand. Given my massive chip lead and the fact that there are only a few hands remaining in the match, there is no need to get involved with subpar holdings. I will simply fold and preserve my stack, avoiding any unnecessary variance.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 85o. This is a very weak hand. I am currently significantly behind the leader (Pro) in chips, and there are only three hands remaining in the tournament. While I need to find opportunities to gain chips, 85o is near the bottom of my range and playing it against a chip leader who has been aggressive and effective post-flop (as seen in Hand #146) is likely to result in further losses. I will fold to preserve my stack for the remaining two hands.

I have 7-2 suited, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With only two hands left in the tournament and a massive chip lead (23,500 vs 6,350), there is absolutely no reason to risk chips with garbage. I will simply fold and preserve my stack.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am in the Small Blind with 10c 3c, a very weak hand. I am significantly trailing 'Pro' in total chips (6,450 vs 23,400). My goal is to maximize my stack size. Given the hand's weakness and the fact that it is the last hand, I will fold to preserve my remaining 6,400 chips rather than entering a pot with a sub-optimal hand against a chip leader who has been playing aggressively.
